**Socialism** is an [economic](/wiki/Economic_ideology "Economic ideology") and [political philosophy](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy") encompassing diverse [economic](/wiki/Economic_system "Economic system") and [social systems](/wiki/Social_system "Social system")[[1]](#cite_note-1) characterised by [social ownership](/wiki/Social_ownership "Social ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) as opposed to [private ownership](/wiki/Private_ownership "Private ownership").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorvat20001515–1516-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArnold19947–8-4)[[5]](#cite_note-oxfordcomp-5) It describes the [economic](/wiki/Economic_ideology "Economic ideology"), [political](/wiki/Political_philosophy "Political philosophy"), and [social](/wiki/Social_theory "Social theory") theories and [movements](/wiki/Political_movement "Political movement") associated with the implementation of such systems.[[6]](#cite_note-Socialism_at_The_Free_dictionary-6) Social ownership can take various forms, including [public](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership"), [community](/wiki/Community_ownership "Community ownership"), [collective](/wiki/Collective_ownership "Collective ownership"), [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative"),[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) or [employee](/wiki/Employee_stock_ownership "Employee stock ownership").[[10]](#cite_note-Horvat_2000-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) As one of the main ideologies on the [political spectrum](/wiki/Political_spectrum "Political spectrum"), socialism is the standard [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing "Left-wing") ideology in most countries.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Types of socialism vary based on the role of markets and planning in [resource allocation](/wiki/Resource_allocation "Resource allocation"), and the structure of management in organizations.[[13]](#cite_note-Nove-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Socialist systems are divided into [non-market](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning") and [market](/wiki/Market_(economics) "Market (economics)") forms.[[15]](#cite_note-Kolb-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) A non-market socialist system seeks to eliminate the perceived [inefficiencies, irrationalities, unpredictability](/wiki/Criticism_of_capitalism#Inefficiency,_irrationality,_and_unpredictability "Criticism of capitalism"), and [crises](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") that socialists traditionally associate with [capital accumulation](/wiki/Capital_accumulation "Capital accumulation") and the [profit](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)") system.[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Market socialism](/wiki/Market_socialism "Market socialism") retains the use of monetary prices, factor markets and sometimes the [profit motive](/wiki/Profit_motive "Profit motive").[[18]](#cite_note-Social_Dividend_versus_Basic_Income_Guarantee_in_Market_Socialism,_2004-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) As a political force, [socialist parties](/wiki/Socialist_parties "Socialist parties") and ideas exercise varying degrees of power and influence, heading national governments in several countries. Socialist politics have been [internationalist](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism") and [nationalist](/wiki/Socialist_nationalism "Socialist nationalism"); organised through political parties and opposed to party politics; at times overlapping with [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") and other times independent and critical of them, and present in industrialised and developing nations.[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") originated within the socialist movement,[[22]](#cite_note-22) supporting [economic](/wiki/Economic_interventionism "Economic interventionism") and [social interventions](/wiki/Social_interventionism "Social interventionism") to promote [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) While retaining socialism as a long-term goal,[[25]](#cite_note-25) in the post-war period social democracy embraced a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") based on [Keynesianism](/wiki/Keynesianism "Keynesianism") within a predominantly developed capitalist [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") and [liberal democratic](/wiki/Liberal_democratic "Liberal democratic") polity that expands state intervention to include [income redistribution](/wiki/Income_redistribution "Income redistribution"), [regulation](/wiki/Regulatory_economics "Regulatory economics"), and a [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The socialist [political movement](/wiki/Political_movement "Political movement") includes political philosophies that originated in the revolutionary movements of the mid-to-late 18th century and out of concern for the [social problems](/wiki/Social_problems "Social problems") that socialists associated with capitalism.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambDocherty20061-28) By the late 19th century, after the work of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and his collaborator [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels"), socialism had come to signify [anti-capitalism](/wiki/Anti-capitalism "Anti-capitalism") and advocacy for a [post-capitalist](/wiki/Post-capitalist "Post-capitalist") system based on some form of social ownership of the means of production.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Anthony_Giddens_1994,_p._71-30) By the early 1920s, [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and social democracy had become the two dominant political tendencies within the international socialist movement,[[31]](#cite_note-31) with socialism itself becoming the most influential secular movement of the 20th century.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Many socialists also adopted the causes of other social movements, such as [feminism](/wiki/Socialist_feminism "Socialist feminism"), [environmentalism](/wiki/Eco-socialism "Eco-socialism"), and [progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Although the emergence of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") as the world's first nominally [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state") led to the widespread association of socialism with the [Soviet economic model](/wiki/Soviet_economic_model "Soviet economic model"), it has since shifted in favour of [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism"). Academics sometimes recognised the [mixed economies](/wiki/Mixed_economies "Mixed economies") of several Western European and Nordic countries as "democratic socialist",[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) although the system of these countries, with only limited social ownership (generally in the form of state ownership), is more usually described as [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy"). Following the [revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989"), many of these countries moved away from socialism as a [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") consensus replaced the social democratic consensus in the advanced capitalist world.[[36]](#cite_note-36) In parallel, many former socialist politicians and political parties embraced "[Third Way](/wiki/Third_Way "Third Way")" politics, remaining committed to equality and welfare while abandoning public ownership and class-based politics.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) Socialism experienced a resurgence in popularity in the 2010s due to the [2008 global financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis").[[38]](#cite_note-:3-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Etymology

According to Andrew Vincent, the word 'socialism' finds its root in the Latin *sociare*, which means to combine or to share. The related, more technical term in Roman and then medieval law was *societas*. This latter word could mean companionship and fellowship as well as the more legalistic idea of a consensual contract between freemen".[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Ehrerbietige_Vorstellung_und_Einladung_an_meine_lieben_Mitmenschen.pdf/page1-250px-Ehrerbietige_Vorstellung_und_Einladung_an_meine_lieben_Mitmenschen.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ehrerbietige_Vorstellung_und_Einladung_an_meine_lieben_Mitmenschen.pdf)

19th century [utopian socialist](/wiki/Utopian_socialist "Utopian socialist") pamphlet by [Rudolf Sutermeister](/wiki/Rudolf_Sutermeister "Rudolf Sutermeister")

Initial use of *socialism* was claimed by [Pierre Leroux](/wiki/Pierre_Leroux "Pierre Leroux"), who alleged he first used the term in the Parisian journal *[Le Globe](/wiki/Le_Globe "Le Globe")* in 1832.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Kołakowski2005-42) Leroux was a follower of [Henri de Saint-Simon](/wiki/Henri_de_Saint-Simon "Henri de Saint-Simon"), one of the founders of what would later be labelled [utopian socialism](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism"). Socialism contrasted with the liberal doctrine of [individualism](/wiki/Liberal_individualism "Liberal individualism") that emphasized the moral worth of the individual while stressing that people act or should act as if they are in isolation from one another. The original utopian socialists condemned this doctrine of individualism for failing to address social concerns during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), including poverty, oppression, and vast [wealth inequality](/wiki/Wealth_inequality "Wealth inequality"). They viewed their society as harming community life by basing society on competition. They presented socialism as an alternative to liberal individualism based on the shared ownership of resources.[[43]](#cite_note-Marvin_Perry_1600,_p._540-43) Saint-Simon proposed [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning"), scientific administration and the application of scientific understanding to the organisation of society. By contrast, [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen") proposed to organise production and ownership via [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative").[[43]](#cite_note-Marvin_Perry_1600,_p._540-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) *Socialism* is also attributed in France to [Marie Roch Louis Reybaud](/wiki/Marie_Roch_Louis_Reybaud "Marie Roch Louis Reybaud") while in Britain it is attributed to Owen, who became one of the fathers of the [cooperative movement](/wiki/Cooperative_movement "Cooperative movement").[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The definition and usage of *socialism* settled by the 1860s, with the term *socialist* replacing *[associationist](/wiki/Associationist "Associationist")*, *[co-operative](/wiki/Co-operative "Co-operative")*, *[mutualist](/wiki/Mutualism_(economic_theory) "Mutualism (economic theory)")* and *[collectivist](/wiki/Collectivist "Collectivist")*, which had been used as synonyms, while the term *communism* fell out of use during this period.[[47]](#cite_note-Williams_1983_288-47) An early distinction between *[communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism")* and *socialism* was that the latter aimed to only socialise production while the former aimed to socialise both [production](/wiki/Production_(economics) "Production (economics)") and [consumption](/wiki/Consumption_(economics) "Consumption (economics)") (in the form of free access to final goods).[[48]](#cite_note-Steele_1992_43-48) By 1888, [Marxists](/wiki/Marxists "Marxists") employed *socialism* in place of *communism* as the latter had come to be considered an old-fashioned synonym for *socialism*. It was not until after the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_Revolution "Bolshevik Revolution") that *socialism* was appropriated by [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") to mean a stage between [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and communism. He used it to defend the Bolshevik program from Marxist criticism that Russia's [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces") were not sufficiently developed for communism.[[49]](#cite_note-Steele_1992_44-45-49) The distinction between *communism* and *socialism* became salient in 1918 after the [Russian Social Democratic Labour Party](/wiki/Russian_Social_Democratic_Labour_Party "Russian Social Democratic Labour Party") renamed itself to the [All-Russian Communist Party](/wiki/All-Russian_Communist_Party "All-Russian Communist Party"), interpreting *communism* specifically to mean socialists who supported the politics and theories of [Bolshevism](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism"), [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") and later that of [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) although [communist parties](/wiki/Communist_parties "Communist parties") continued to describe themselves as socialists dedicated to socialism.[[51]](#cite_note-51) According to *The Oxford Handbook of Karl Marx*, "Marx used many terms to refer to a [post-capitalist society](/wiki/Post-capitalist_society "Post-capitalist society")—positive [humanism](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism"), socialism, communism, realm of free individuality, [free association of producers](/wiki/Free_association_of_producers "Free association of producers"), etc. He used these terms completely interchangeably. The notion that 'socialism' and 'communism' are distinct historical stages is alien to his work and only entered the lexicon of Marxism after his death".[[52]](#cite_note-Hudis-52)

In [Christian Europe](/wiki/Christian_Europe "Christian Europe"), communists were believed to have adopted [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"). In Protestant England, *communism* was too close to the [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [communion rite](/wiki/Communion_rite "Communion rite"), hence *socialist* was the preferred term.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Engels wrote that in 1848, when *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* was published, socialism was respectable in Europe while communism was not. The [Owenites](/wiki/Owenites "Owenites") in England and the [Fourierists](/wiki/Fourierists "Fourierists") in France were considered respectable socialists while working-class movements that "proclaimed the necessity of total social change" denoted themselves *communists*.[[54]](#cite_note-54) This branch of socialism produced the communist work of [Étienne Cabet](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Cabet "Étienne Cabet") in France and [Wilhelm Weitling](/wiki/Wilhelm_Weitling "Wilhelm Weitling") in Germany.[[55]](#cite_note-55) British moral philosopher [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill") discussed a form of economic socialism within free market. In later editions of his *[Principles of Political Economy](/wiki/Principles_of_Political_Economy "Principles of Political Economy")* (1848), Mill posited that "as far as economic theory was concerned, there is nothing in principle in economic theory that precludes an economic order based on socialist policies"[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) and promoted substituting capitalist businesses with [worker cooperatives](/wiki/Worker_cooperative "Worker cooperative").[[58]](#cite_note-58) While democrats looked to the [Revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848") as a [democratic revolution](/wiki/Democratic_revolution "Democratic revolution") which in the long run ensured [liberty, equality, and fraternity](/wiki/Liberty,_equality,_and_fraternity "Liberty, equality, and fraternity"), Marxists denounced it as a betrayal of working-class ideals by a [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") indifferent to the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat").[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## History

Main article: [History of socialism](/wiki/History_of_socialism "History of socialism")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **appears to be [slanted towards recent events](/wiki/Wikipedia:Recentism "Wikipedia:Recentism").** Please try to keep recent events in historical perspective and [add more content](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Socialism&action=edit) related to non-recent events. *(January 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/New_Harmony%2C_Indiana%2C_por_F._Bates.jpg/330px-New_Harmony%2C_Indiana%2C_por_F._Bates.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Harmony,_Indiana,_por_F._Bates.jpg)

New Harmony, a utopian attempt as proposed by [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen")

The history of socialism has its origins in the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") and the 1789 [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), along with the changes that brought, although it has precedents in earlier movements and ideas. *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* was written by [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") in 1847–48 just before the [Revolutions of 1848](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848") swept Europe, expressing what they termed [scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism"). In the last third of the 19th century parties dedicated to [Democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") arose in Europe, drawing mainly from [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism"). For a duration of one week in December 1899, the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") formed the first socialist government in the world when it was elected into power in the [Colony of Queensland](/wiki/Colony_of_Queensland "Colony of Queensland") with Premier [Anderson Dawson](/wiki/Anderson_Dawson "Anderson Dawson") as its leader.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### 20th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Communist_Block.svg/500px-Communist_Block.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Communist_Block.svg)

States that had communist governments

States that the Soviet Union believed at one point to be [moving toward socialism](/wiki/Moving_toward_socialism "Moving toward socialism")

Other states allied with the Soviet Union at some point

In the first half of the 20th century, the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and the [communist parties](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party") of the [Third International](/wiki/Third_International "Third International") around the world, came to represent socialism in terms of the [Soviet model of economic development](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Soviet_Union "Economy of the Soviet Union") and the creation of [centrally planned economies](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") directed by a state that owns all the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), although other trends condemned what they saw as the lack of democracy. The establishment of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") in 1949, saw socialism introduced. China established a self-sufficient and self-reliant economy that was state-led and focused on rapid industrialization.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Other policies included land redistribution and the [Anti-Rightist Movement](/wiki/Anti-Rightist_Movement "Anti-Rightist Movement"), followed by the disastrous [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward"). In the UK, [Herbert Morrison](/wiki/Herbert_Morrison "Herbert Morrison") said that "socialism is what the [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") government does" whereas [Aneurin Bevan](/wiki/Aneurin_Bevan "Aneurin Bevan") argued socialism requires that the "main streams of economic activity are brought under public direction", with an economic plan and workers' democracy.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Some argued that capitalism had been abolished.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Socialist governments established the [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") with partial [nationalisations](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation") and social welfare.

By 1968, the prolonged [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") gave rise to the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left"), socialists who tended to be critical of the Soviet Union and [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy"). [Anarcho-syndicalists](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalists "Anarcho-syndicalists") and some elements of the New Left and others favoured [decentralised](/wiki/Decentralized_planning "Decentralized planning") [collective ownership](/wiki/Collective_ownership "Collective ownership") in the form of [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperatives "Cooperatives") or [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_councils "Workers' councils"). In 1989, the Soviet Union saw the end of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"), marked by the [Revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") across [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe"), culminating in the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991. Socialists have adopted the causes of other social movements such as [environmentalism](/wiki/Eco-socialism "Eco-socialism"), [feminism](/wiki/Socialist_feminism "Socialist feminism") and [progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Historian William Blum highlights that the historical record of twentieth-century socialist experiments cannot be evaluated in isolation from external interference. Blum argues that:

> [E]very socialist experiment of any significance in the twentieth century – without exception – has either been crushed, overthrown, or invaded, or corrupted, perverted, subverted, or destabilized, or otherwise had life made impossible for it, by the United States. Not one socialist government or movement – from the Russian Revolution to the Sandinistas in Nicaragua, from Communist China to the FMLN in Salvador – not one was permitted to rise or fall solely on its own merits; not one was left secure enough to drop its guard against the all-powerful enemy abroad and freely and fully relax control at home.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Early 21st century

See also: [Socialism of the 21st century](/wiki/Socialism_of_the_21st_century "Socialism of the 21st century")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Foro_sao_paulo_members_2023.svg/250px-Foro_sao_paulo_members_2023.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Foro_sao_paulo_members_2023.svg)

Map showing socialism in Latin America, the [pink tide](/wiki/Pink_tide "Pink tide") (Foro de São Paulo members) in 2023:

Socialist government

Member not in government

In 1990, the [São Paulo Forum](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_Forum "São Paulo Forum") was launched by the [Workers' Party (Brazil)](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_(Brazil) "Workers' Party (Brazil)"), linking left-wing socialist parties in Latin America. Its members were associated with the [pink tide](/wiki/Pink_tide "Pink tide") of left-wing governments on the continent in the early 21st century. Member parties ruling countries included the [Front for Victory](/wiki/Front_for_Victory "Front for Victory") in Argentina, the [PAIS Alliance](/wiki/PAIS_Alliance "PAIS Alliance") in Ecuador, [Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front](/wiki/Farabundo_Mart%C3%AD_National_Liberation_Front "Farabundo Martí National Liberation Front") in El Salvador, [Peru Wins](/wiki/Peru_Wins "Peru Wins") in Peru, and the [United Socialist Party of Venezuela](/wiki/United_Socialist_Party_of_Venezuela "United Socialist Party of Venezuela"), whose leader [Hugo Chávez](/wiki/Hugo_Ch%C3%A1vez "Hugo Chávez") initiated what he called "[Socialism of the 21st century](/wiki/Socialism_of_the_21st_century "Socialism of the 21st century")".

Many mainstream democratic socialist and social democratic parties continued to drift right-wards. On the right of the socialist movement, the [Progressive Alliance](/wiki/Progressive_Alliance "Progressive Alliance") was founded in 2013 by current or former members of the [Socialist International](/wiki/Socialist_International "Socialist International"). The organisation states the aim of becoming the global network of "the [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism"), [democratic](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism"), [social-democratic](/wiki/Social-democratic "Social-democratic"), socialist and [labour movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement")".[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) Mainstream social democratic and socialist parties are also networked in Europe in the [Party of European Socialists](/wiki/Party_of_European_Socialists "Party of European Socialists") formed in 1992. Many of these parties lost large parts of their electoral base in the early 21st century. This phenomenon is known as [Pasokification](/wiki/Pasokification "Pasokification")[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) from the Greek party [PASOK](/wiki/PASOK "PASOK"), which saw a declining share of the vote in national elections—from 43.9% in 2009 to 13.2% in May 2012, to 12.3% in June 2012 and 4.7% in 2015—due to its poor handling of the [Greek government-debt crisis](/wiki/Greek_government-debt_crisis "Greek government-debt crisis") and implementation of harsh [austerity](/wiki/Austerity "Austerity") measures.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In Europe, the share of votes for such socialist parties was at its 70-year lowest in 2015. For example, the [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(France) "Socialist Party (France)"), after winning the [2012 French presidential election](/wiki/2012_French_presidential_election "2012 French presidential election"), rapidly lost its vote share, the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany")'s fortunes declined rapidly from 2005 to 2019, and outside Europe the [Israeli Labor Party](/wiki/Israeli_Labor_Party "Israeli Labor Party") fell from being the dominant force in Israeli politics to 4.43% of the vote in the [April 2019 Israeli legislative election](/wiki/April_2019_Israeli_legislative_election "April 2019 Israeli legislative election"), and the [Peruvian Aprista Party](/wiki/Peruvian_Aprista_Party "Peruvian Aprista Party") went from ruling party in 2011 to a minor party. The decline of these mainstream parties opened space for more radical and populist left parties in some countries, such as Spain's [Podemos](/wiki/Podemos_(Spanish_political_party) "Podemos (Spanish political party)"), Greece's [Syriza](/wiki/Syriza "Syriza") (in government, 2015–19), Germany's [Die Linke](/wiki/Die_Linke "Die Linke"), and France's [La France Insoumise](/wiki/La_France_Insoumise "La France Insoumise"). In other countries, left-wing revivals have taken place within mainstream democratic socialist and centrist parties, as with [Jeremy Corbyn](/wiki/Jeremy_Corbyn "Jeremy Corbyn") in the United Kingdom and [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") in the United States.[[38]](#cite_note-:3-38) Few of these radical left parties have won national government in Europe, while some more mainstream socialist parties have managed to, such as Portugal's [Socialist Party](/wiki/Socialist_Party_(Portugal) "Socialist Party (Portugal)").[[72]](#cite_note-72)

[Bhaskar Sunkara](/wiki/Bhaskar_Sunkara "Bhaskar Sunkara"), the founding editor of the American socialist magazine [*Jacobin*](/wiki/Jacobin_(magazine) "Jacobin (magazine)"), argued that the appeal of socialism persists due to the inequality and "tremendous suffering" under current global capitalism, the use of wage labor "which rests on the exploitation and domination of humans by other humans," and ecological crises, such as [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[73]](#cite_note-:2-73) In contrast, [Mark J. Perry](/wiki/Mark_J._Perry "Mark J. Perry") of the conservative [American Enterprise Institute](/wiki/American_Enterprise_Institute "American Enterprise Institute") (AEI) argued that despite socialism's resurgence, it is still "a flawed system based on completely faulty principles that aren't consistent with human behavior and can't nurture the human spirit.", adding that "While it promised prosperity, equality, and security, it delivered poverty, misery, and tyranny."[[74]](#cite_note-74) Some in the scientific community have suggested that a contemporary radical response to social and ecological problems could be seen in the emergence of movements associated with [degrowth](/wiki/Degrowth "Degrowth"), [eco-socialism](/wiki/Eco-socialism "Eco-socialism") and [eco-anarchism](/wiki/Eco-anarchism "Eco-anarchism").[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## Varieties

See also: [Labour movement](/wiki/Labour_movement "Labour movement"), [The labor problem](/wiki/The_labor_problem "The labor problem"), and [Trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Socialists_in_Union_Square%2C_N.Y.C._%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Socialists_in_Union_Square%2C_N.Y.C._%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Socialists_in_Union_Square,_N.Y.C._(cropped).jpg)

Socialists in [Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square,_Manhattan "Union Square, Manhattan"), New York City on [May Day](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") 1912

While major socialist political movements include anarchism, communism, the labour movement, Marxism, social democracy, and syndicalism, independent socialist theorists, [utopian socialist](/wiki/Utopian_socialist "Utopian socialist") authors, and academic supporters of socialism may not be represented in these movements. Some political groups have called themselves *socialist* while holding views that some consider antithetical to socialism. *Socialist* has been used by members of the [political right](/wiki/Political_right "Political right") as an epithet, including against individuals who do not consider themselves to be socialists and against policies that are not considered socialist by their proponents. While there are many variations of socialism, and there is no single definition encapsulating all of socialism, there have been common elements identified by scholars.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambDocherty20061–3-77)

In his *Dictionary of Socialism* (1924), Angelo S. Rappoport analysed forty definitions of socialism to conclude that common elements of socialism include general criticism of the social effects of [private ownership](/wiki/Private_ownership "Private ownership") and control of capital—as being the cause of poverty, low wages, unemployment, economic and social inequality and a lack of economic security; a general view that the solution to these problems is a form of collective control over the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), [distribution](/wiki/Distribution_(economics) "Distribution (economics)") and [exchange](/wiki/Means_of_exchange "Means of exchange") (the degree and means of control vary among socialist movements); an agreement that the outcome of this collective control should be a society based upon [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"), including social equality, economic protection of people and should provide a more satisfying life for most people.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambDocherty20061–2-78)

In *The Concepts of Socialism* (1975), [Bhikhu Parekh](/wiki/Bhikhu_Parekh "Bhikhu Parekh") identifies four core principles of socialism and particularly socialist society, namely sociality, social responsibility, cooperation and planning.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambDocherty20062-79) In his study *Ideologies and Political Theory* (1996), [Michael Freeden](/wiki/Michael_Freeden "Michael Freeden") states that all socialists share five themes: the first is that socialism posits that society is more than a mere collection of individuals; second, that it considers human welfare a desirable objective; third, that it considers humans by nature to be active and productive; fourth, it holds the belief of human equality; and fifth, that history is progressive and will create positive change on the condition that humans work to achieve such change.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambDocherty20062-79)

### African socialism

Main article: [African socialism](/wiki/African_socialism "African socialism")

[![This map shows the 35 countries that adopted some form of socialism on the African continent from the 1950s to the 1980s using modern boundaries.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/African_socialism_1950s_to_1980s.svg/250px-African_socialism_1950s_to_1980s.svg.png)](/wiki/File:African_socialism_1950s_to_1980s.svg)

This map shows the 35 countries that adopted some form of socialism on the African continent from the 1950s to the 1980s.[[80]](#cite_note-:152-80)

African socialism is a distinct variant of socialist theory developed in post-colonial Africa during the mid-20th century. As a shared ideology among several African thinkers over the decades, it encompasses a variety of competing interpretations. However, a consistent and defining theme among these theories is the notion that traditional African cultures and community structures have a natural inclination toward socialist principles.

Several African countries adopted socialist systems from the 1950s to mid-1980s (~35 out of ~50-53 countries at the time).[[80]](#cite_note-:152-80)

### Anarchism

Main article: [Anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism")

[![A black capital letter A inside a black ring. The edges of the A do not go outside the ring, they smoothly fit into it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Anarchy-symbol.svg/250px-Anarchy-symbol.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Anarchy-symbol.svg)

The capital letter *[A](/wiki/A "A")* surrounded by a [circle](/wiki/Circle "Circle") is a common symbol of anarchism.

Anarchism advocates [stateless societies](/wiki/Stateless_societies "Stateless societies") often defined as [self-governed](/wiki/Self-governed "Self-governed") voluntary institutions,[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) but that several authors have defined as more specific institutions based on non-[hierarchical](/wiki/Hierarchical "Hierarchical") [free associations](/wiki/Free_association_(communism_and_anarchism) "Free association (communism and anarchism)").[[85]](#cite_note-iaf-ifa.org-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) While anarchism holds the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") to be undesirable, unnecessary or harmful,[[89]](#cite_note-definition-89)[[90]](#cite_note-slevin-90) it is not the central aspect.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLaughlin200728-91) Anarchism entails opposing [authority](/wiki/Authority "Authority") or [hierarchical organisation](/wiki/Hierarchical_organisation "Hierarchical organisation") in the conduct of human relations, including the state system.[[85]](#cite_note-iaf-ifa.org-85)[[92]](#cite_note-Brown_2002_106-92)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLaughlin20071-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) [Mutualists](/wiki/Mutualism_(economic_theory) "Mutualism (economic theory)") support market socialism, [collectivist anarchists](/wiki/Collectivist_anarchist "Collectivist anarchist") favour [workers cooperatives](/wiki/Workers_cooperative "Workers cooperative") and salaries based on the amount of time contributed to production, [anarcho-communists](/wiki/Anarcho-communists "Anarcho-communists") advocate a direct transition from capitalism to [libertarian communism](/wiki/Libertarian_communism "Libertarian communism") and a [gift economy](/wiki/Gift_economy "Gift economy") and [anarcho-syndicalists](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalist "Anarcho-syndicalist") prefer workers' [direct action](/wiki/Direct_action "Direct action") and the [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike").[[96]](#cite_note-McKay_2008-96)

The authoritarian–[libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian") struggles and disputes within the socialist movement go back to the First International and the expulsion in 1872 of the anarchists, who went on to lead the [Anti-authoritarian International](/wiki/Anti-authoritarian_International "Anti-authoritarian International") and then founded their own libertarian international, the [Anarchist St. Imier International](/wiki/Anarchist_St._Imier_International "Anarchist St. Imier International").[[97]](#cite_note-97) In 1888, the [individualist anarchist](/wiki/Individualist_anarchist "Individualist anarchist") [Benjamin Tucker](/wiki/Benjamin_Tucker "Benjamin Tucker"), who proclaimed himself to be an anarchistic socialist and [libertarian socialist](/wiki/Libertarian_socialist "Libertarian socialist") in opposition to the authoritarian [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") and the compulsory communism, included the full text of a "Socialistic Letter" by [Ernest Lesigne](/wiki/Ernest_Lesigne "Ernest Lesigne")[[98]](#cite_note-98) in his essay on "State Socialism and Anarchism". According to Lesigne, there are two types of socialism: "One is dictatorial, the other libertarian".[[99]](#cite_note-99) Tucker's two socialisms were the authoritarian state socialism which he associated to the Marxist school and the libertarian anarchist socialism, or simply anarchism, that he advocated. Tucker noted that the fact that the authoritarian "State Socialism has overshadowed other forms of Socialism gives it no right to a monopoly of the Socialistic idea".[[100]](#cite_note-100) According to Tucker, what those two schools of socialism had in common was the [labor theory of value](/wiki/Labor_theory_of_value "Labor theory of value") and the ends, by which anarchism pursued different means.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

According to anarchists such as the authors of *An Anarchist FAQ*, anarchism is one of the many traditions of socialism. For anarchists and other anti-authoritarian socialists, socialism "can only mean a classless and anti-authoritarian (i.e. libertarian) society in which people manage their own affairs, either as individuals or as part of a group (depending on the situation). In other words, it implies self-management in all aspects of life", including at the workplace.[[96]](#cite_note-McKay_2008-96) Michael Newman includes anarchism as one of many socialist traditions.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman2005-102) [Peter Marshall](/wiki/Peter_Marshall_(author,_born_1946) "Peter Marshall (author, born 1946)") argues that "[i]n general anarchism is closer to socialism than liberalism. ... Anarchism finds itself largely in the socialist camp, but it also has outriders in liberalism. It cannot be reduced to socialism, and is best seen as a separate and distinctive doctrine."[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### Arab socialism

Main article: [Arab socialism](/wiki/Arab_socialism "Arab socialism")

Arab socialism ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الإشتِراكيّة العربية, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Al-Ishtirākīya Al-'Arabīya*) is a political ideology based on the combination of [pan-Arabism](/wiki/Pan-Arabism "Pan-Arabism") or [Arab nationalism](/wiki/Arab_nationalism "Arab nationalism") and socialism. The term *Arab socialism* was coined by [Michel Aflaq](/wiki/Michel_Aflaq "Michel Aflaq"),[[104]](#cite_note-:02-104) the principal founder of [Ba'athism](/wiki/Ba%27athism "Ba'athism") and the [Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party](/wiki/Ba%27ath_Party "Ba'ath Party") in [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), to distinguish his version of socialist ideology from the international socialist movement. While distinct from the much broader tradition of socialist thought in the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world"), which predates Arab socialism by as much as fifty years, direct influence and evolutions of his thought, [Marxist](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism") or otherwise, were realized and expanded upon in countries like Syria, [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") and others.[[105]](#cite_note-:6-105)[[106]](#cite_note-:7-106)[[107]](#cite_note-:8-107) A main innovation of Aflaq's thought was the transformation of Arab unity from an intellectual ideal into a real-world political pursuit of rights alongside a new set of socioeconomic conditions.[[105]](#cite_note-:6-105) [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser")'s Egypt in particular served as a potent vehicle for Aflaq's thought, and would have to grapple with crises in the intellectual and military spheres.[[106]](#cite_note-:7-106)[[107]](#cite_note-:8-107)[[108]](#cite_note-:9-108)

### Authoritarian socialism

[Authoritarian socialism](/wiki/Authoritarian_socialism "Authoritarian socialism"), or socialism from above,[[109]](#cite_note-Authoritarian_socialism_Two_Souls-109) is an [economic](/wiki/Economic_system "Economic system") and [political system](/wiki/Political_system "Political system") supporting some form of [socialist economics](/wiki/Socialist_economics "Socialist economics") while rejecting [political pluralism](/wiki/Pluralism_(political_philosophy) "Pluralism (political philosophy)"). As a term, it represents a set of economic-political systems describing themselves as "socialist" and rejecting the [liberal-democratic](/wiki/Liberal-democratic "Liberal-democratic") concepts of [multi-party politics](/wiki/Multi-party_politics "Multi-party politics"), [freedom of assembly](/wiki/Freedom_of_assembly "Freedom of assembly"), *[habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus"),* and [freedom of expression](/wiki/Freedom_of_expression "Freedom of expression"), either due to fear of [counter-revolution](/wiki/Counter-revolution "Counter-revolution") or as a means to [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") ends. Journalists and scholars have characterised [several countries](/wiki/List_of_non-communist_socialist_states "List of non-communist socialist states"), most notably the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), and their allies, as authoritarian socialist states.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHuntington1970-110)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELöwy1986264-111)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmandae2003-112)

#### Leninism

Main articles: [Blanquism](/wiki/Blanquism "Blanquism"), [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism"), [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism"), and [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Lenin_in_1920_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Lenin_in_1920_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lenin_in_1920_(cropped).jpg)

Russian revolutionary, politician, and political theorist [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") in 1920

Blanquism is a conception of revolution named for [Louis Auguste Blanqui](/wiki/Louis_Auguste_Blanqui "Louis Auguste Blanqui"). It holds that socialist revolution should be carried out by a relatively small group of highly organised and secretive conspirators. Upon seizing power, the revolutionaries introduce socialism.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Rosa Luxemburg](/wiki/Rosa_Luxemburg "Rosa Luxemburg") and [Eduard Bernstein](/wiki/Eduard_Bernstein "Eduard Bernstein")[[113]](#cite_note-bern-113) criticised Lenin, stating that his conception of revolution was elitist and Blanquist.[[114]](#cite_note-114) Marxism–Leninism combines Marx's [scientific socialist](/wiki/Scientific_socialist "Scientific socialist") concepts and Lenin's [anti-imperialism](/wiki/Anti-imperialism "Anti-imperialism"), [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism"), [vanguardism](/wiki/Vanguardism "Vanguardism")[[115]](#cite_note-115) and the principle of "[He who does not work, neither shall he eat](/wiki/He_who_does_not_work,_neither_shall_he_eat "He who does not work, neither shall he eat")".[[116]](#cite_note-116)

[Hal Draper](/wiki/Hal_Draper "Hal Draper") defined [socialism from above](/wiki/Socialism_from_above "Socialism from above") as the philosophy which employs an elite [administration](/wiki/Administration_(government) "Administration (government)") to run the [socialist state](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state"). The other side of socialism is a more democratic [socialism from below](/wiki/Socialism_from_below "Socialism from below").[[117]](#cite_note-Two_Souls-117) The idea of socialism from above is much more frequently discussed in elite circles than socialism from below—even if that is the Marxist ideal—because it is more practical.[[118]](#cite_note-118) Draper viewed socialism from below as being the purer, more [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") version of socialism.[[119]](#cite_note-119) According to Draper, [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") were devoutly opposed to any socialist institution that was "conducive to superstitious authoritarianism". Draper makes the argument that this division echoes the division between "reformist or revolutionary, peaceful or violent, democratic or authoritarian, etc." and further identifies six major varieties of socialism from above, among them "Philanthropism", "Elitism", "Pannism", "Communism", "Permeationism" and "Socialism-from-Outside".[[120]](#cite_note-120)
According to Arthur Lipow, Marx and Engels were "the founders of modern revolutionary democratic socialism", described as a form of "socialism from below" that is "based on a mass working-class movement, fighting from below for the extension of democracy and human freedom". This [type of socialism](/wiki/Type_of_socialism "Type of socialism") is contrasted to that of the "authoritarian, anti-democratic creed" and "the various totalitarian collectivist ideologies which claim the title of socialism" as well as "the many varieties of 'socialism from above' which have led in the twentieth century to movements and state forms in which a despotic '[new class](/wiki/New_class "New class")' rules over a stratified economy in the name of socialism", a division that "runs through the history of the socialist movement". Lipow identifies [Bellamyism](/wiki/Bellamyism "Bellamyism") and [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") as two prominent authoritarian socialist currents within the history of the socialist movement.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

Trotsky viewed himself to be an adherent of Leninism but opposed the [bureaucratic](/wiki/Bureaucratic "Bureaucratic") and authoritarian methods of Stalin.[[122]](#cite_note-122) A number of scholars and Western socialists have regarded Leon Trotsky as a [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") [alternative](/wiki/Anti-Stalinist_Left "Anti-Stalinist Left") rather than a forerunner to Stalin with particular emphasis drawn to his activities in the [pre-Civil War period](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Soviet "Saint Petersburg Soviet") and as leader of the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition").[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126) More specifically, Trotsky advocated for a [decentralised](/wiki/Decentralised "Decentralised") form of economic planning,[[127]](#cite_note-127) mass [soviet](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") [democratization](/wiki/Democratization "Democratization"),[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978[httpsarchiveorgdetailssocialpoliticalt0000kneipage300mode2upqproletarianculture_207–215]-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) [worker's control of production](/wiki/Worker%27s_control "Worker's control"),[[131]](#cite_note-131) elected representation of Soviet [socialist parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union"),[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher2015[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidYGznDwAAQBAJqisaacdeutschertrotskytheprophet_293]-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133) the tactic of a [united front](/wiki/United_front "United front") against far-right parties,[[134]](#cite_note-134) [cultural](/wiki/Cultural "Cultural") autonomy for artistic movements,[[135]](#cite_note-135) voluntary [collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivisation "Collectivisation"),[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) a [transitional program](/wiki/The_Death_Agony_of_Capitalism_and_the_Tasks_of_the_Fourth_International "The Death Agony of Capitalism and the Tasks of the Fourth International"),[[138]](#cite_note-138) and socialist [internationalism](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism").[[139]](#cite_note-139)

Several scholars state that in practice, the Soviet model functioned as a form of [state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism").[[140]](#cite_note-Chomsky_1986-140)[[141]](#cite_note-State_Capitalism_in_the_Soviet_Union,_2001-141)[[142]](#cite_note-Fitzgibbons_2002-142)

### Democratic socialism and social democracy

Main articles: [Democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") and [Social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Pu%C3%B1o_y_rosa.svg/250px-Pu%C3%B1o_y_rosa.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pu%C3%B1o_y_rosa.svg)

The [fist and rose](/wiki/Fist_and_rose "Fist and rose"), a [common symbol](/wiki/Rose_symbolism#Socialism "Rose symbolism") of democratic socialism and social democracy

> You can't talk about ending the slums without first saying profit must be taken out of slums. You're really tampering and getting on dangerous ground because you are messing with folk then. You are messing with captains of industry. Now this means that we are treading in difficult water, because it really means that we are saying that something is wrong with capitalism. There must be a better distribution of wealth, and maybe America must move toward a democratic socialism.[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)

—[Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr."), 1966

Democratic socialism represents any socialist movement that seeks to establish an economy based on [economic democracy](/wiki/Economic_democracy "Economic democracy") by and for the working class. Democratic socialism is difficult to define and groups of scholars have radically different definitions for the term. Some definitions simply refer to all forms of socialism that follow an electoral, reformist or evolutionary path to socialism rather than a revolutionary one.[[145]](#cite_note-145) According to Christopher Pierson, "[i]f the contrast which 1989 highlights is not that between socialism in the East and liberal democracy in the West, the latter must be recognised to have been shaped, reformed and compromised by a century of social democratic pressure". Pierson further claims that "social democratic and socialist parties within the constitutional arena in the West have almost always been involved in a politics of compromise with existing capitalist institutions (to whatever far distant prize its eyes might from time to time have been lifted)". For Pierson, "if advocates of the death of socialism accept that social democrats belong within the socialist camp, as I think they must, then the contrast between socialism (in all its variants) and liberal democracy must collapse. For *actually existing* liberal democracy is, in substantial part, a product of socialist (social democratic) forces".[[146]](#cite_note-146)

Social democracy is a socialist tradition of political thought.[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman20055-148) Many social democrats refer to themselves as socialists or democratic socialists and some such as [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") employ these terms interchangeably.[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151) Others found "clear differences" between the three terms and prefer to describe their own political beliefs by using the term *social democracy*.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrandalBratbergThorsen20137-152) The two main directions were to establish democratic socialism or to build first a welfare state within the capitalist system. The first variant advances democratic socialism through [reformist](/wiki/Reformist "Reformist") and [gradualist](/wiki/Gradualist "Gradualist") methods.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusky20008-153) In the second variant, social democracy is a policy regime involving a welfare state, [collective bargaining](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") schemes, support for publicly financed public services and a mixed economy. It is often used in this manner to refer to Western and Northern Europe during the later half of the 20th century.[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-Foundations_of_social_democracy,_2009-155) It was described by [Jerry Mander](/wiki/Jerry_Mander "Jerry Mander") as "hybrid economics", an active collaboration of capitalist and socialist visions.[[156]](#cite_note-156) Some studies and surveys indicate that people tend to live happier and healthier lives in social democratic societies rather than [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") ones.[[157]](#cite_note-157)[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160)

Social democrats advocate a peaceful, evolutionary transition of the economy to socialism through [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism") social reform.[[161]](#cite_note-EB-161)[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidkN8llUabGh8CpgPA74_74]-162) It asserts that the only acceptable constitutional form of government is [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") under the rule of law.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeyer201391-163) It promotes extending democratic decision-making beyond political democracy to include [economic democracy](/wiki/Economic_democracy "Economic democracy") to guarantee employees and other economic stakeholders sufficient rights of [co-determination](/wiki/Co-determination "Co-determination").[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeyer201391-163) It supports a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") that opposes inequality, poverty and oppression while rejecting both a totally unregulated [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") or a fully [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy").[[164]](#cite_note-164) Common social democratic policies include universal social rights and universally accessible public services such as education, health care, workers' compensation and other services, including child care and elder care.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeyer2013137-165) Socialist child care and elderly care systems allow citizens to take a more active role in building a socialist society, especially women.[[166]](#cite_note-166) Social democracy supports the trade union labour movement and supports collective bargaining rights for workers.[[167]](#cite_note-167) Most social democratic parties are affiliated with the [Socialist International](/wiki/Socialist_International "Socialist International").[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBusky20008-153)

Modern democratic socialism is a broad political movement that seeks to promote the ideals of socialism within the context of a democratic system. Some democratic socialists support social democracy as a temporary measure to reform the current system while others reject reformism in favour of more revolutionary methods. Modern social democracy emphasises a program of gradual legislative modification of capitalism to make it more equitable and humane while the theoretical end goal of building a socialist society is relegated to the indefinite future. According to [Sheri Berman](/wiki/Sheri_Berman "Sheri Berman"), Marxism is loosely held to be valuable for its emphasis on changing the world for a more just, better future.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

The two movements are widely similar both in terminology and in ideology, although there are a few key differences. The major difference between social democracy and democratic socialism is the object of their politics in that contemporary social democrats support a welfare state and unemployment insurance as well as other practical, progressive reforms of capitalism and are more concerned to administrate and humanise it. On the other hand, democratic socialists seek to replace capitalism with a socialist economic system, arguing that any attempt to humanise capitalism through regulations and welfare policies would distort the market and create economic contradictions.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

### Ethical and liberal socialism

Main articles: [Ethical socialism](/wiki/Ethical_socialism "Ethical socialism") and [Liberal socialism](/wiki/Liberal_socialism "Liberal socialism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/R._H._Tawney.jpg/250px-R._H._Tawney.jpg)](/wiki/File:R._H._Tawney.jpg)

[R. H. Tawney](/wiki/R._H._Tawney "R. H. Tawney"), founder of ethical socialism

Ethical socialism appeals to socialism on ethical and moral grounds as opposed to economic, egoistic, and consumeristic grounds. It emphasizes the need for a morally conscious economy based upon the principles of altruism, cooperation, and social justice while opposing possessive individualism.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson200652,_58–59-170) Ethical socialism has been the official philosophy of mainstream socialist parties.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOrlow2000190TanseyJackson200897-171)

Liberal socialism incorporates liberal principles to socialism.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGausKukathas2004420-172) It has been compared to [post-war social democracy](/wiki/Social_democratic_mixed_economy "Social democratic mixed economy")[[173]](#cite_note-Adams1998-173) for its support of a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy") that includes both public and private capital goods.[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175) While [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") and [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") are [anti-capitalist](/wiki/Anti-capitalist "Anti-capitalist") positions insofar as [criticism of capitalism](/wiki/Criticism_of_capitalism "Criticism of capitalism") is linked to the [private ownership](/wiki/Private_ownership "Private ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"),[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambDocherty20061–2-78) liberal socialism identifies artificial and legalistic monopolies to be the fault of [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism")[[176]](#cite_note-176) and opposes an entirely unregulated [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy").[[177]](#cite_note-ref72-177) It considers both [liberty](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty") and [social equality](/wiki/Social_equality "Social equality") to be compatible and mutually dependent.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGausKukathas2004420-172)

Principles that can be described as ethical or liberal socialist have been based upon or developed by philosophers such as [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill"), [Eduard Bernstein](/wiki/Eduard_Bernstein "Eduard Bernstein"), [John Dewey](/wiki/John_Dewey "John Dewey"), [Carlo Rosselli](/wiki/Carlo_Rosselli "Carlo Rosselli"), [Norberto Bobbio](/wiki/Norberto_Bobbio "Norberto Bobbio"), and [Chantal Mouffe](/wiki/Chantal_Mouffe "Chantal Mouffe").[[178]](#cite_note-178) Other important liberal socialist figures include Guido Calogero, [Piero Gobetti](/wiki/Piero_Gobetti "Piero Gobetti"), [Leonard Trelawny Hobhouse](/wiki/Leonard_Trelawny_Hobhouse "Leonard Trelawny Hobhouse"), [John Maynard Keynes](/wiki/John_Maynard_Keynes "John Maynard Keynes") and [R. H. Tawney](/wiki/R._H._Tawney "R. H. Tawney").[[177]](#cite_note-ref72-177) Liberal socialism has been particularly prominent in British and Italian politics.[[177]](#cite_note-ref72-177)

### Libertarian socialism

Main article: [Libertarian socialism](/wiki/Libertarian_socialism "Libertarian socialism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Le_libertaire_25.png/250px-Le_libertaire_25.png)](/wiki/File:Le_libertaire_25.png)

The first anarchist journal to use the term *[libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian")* was *[Le Libertaire](/wiki/Le_Libertaire "Le Libertaire"), Journal du Mouvement Social*, published in New York City between 1858 and 1861 by French [libertarian communist](/wiki/Libertarian_communist "Libertarian communist") [Joseph Déjacque](/wiki/Joseph_D%C3%A9jacque "Joseph Déjacque"),[[179]](#cite_note-theanarchistlibrary-179) the first recorded person to describe himself as *libertarian.*[[180]](#cite_note-Dejacque-180)

Libertarian socialism, sometimes called [left-libertarianism](/wiki/Left-libertarianism "Left-libertarianism"),[[181]](#cite_note-181)[[182]](#cite_note-182) [social anarchism](/wiki/Social_anarchism "Social anarchism")[[183]](#cite_note-Ostergaard_1991._p._21-183)[[184]](#cite_note-Noam_Chomsky_2004,_p._739-184) and [socialist libertarianism](/wiki/Socialist_libertarianism "Socialist libertarianism"),[[185]](#cite_note-185) is an [anti-authoritarian](/wiki/Anti-authoritarian "Anti-authoritarian"), [anti-statist](/wiki/Anti-statist "Anti-statist") and [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian")[[186]](#cite_note-186) tradition within socialism that rejects centralised state ownership and control[[187]](#cite_note-187) including criticism of [wage labour](/wiki/Wage_labour "Wage labour") relationships ([wage slavery](/wiki/Wage_slavery "Wage slavery"))[[188]](#cite_note-188) as well as the state itself.[[189]](#cite_note-:0-189) It emphasises [workers' self-management](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management") and [decentralised](/wiki/Libertarian_socialist_decentralization "Libertarian socialist decentralization") structures of political organisation.[[189]](#cite_note-:0-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190) Libertarian socialism asserts that a society based on freedom and equality can be achieved through abolishing authoritarian institutions that control production.[[191]](#cite_note-191) Libertarian socialists generally prefer [direct democracy](/wiki/Direct_democracy "Direct democracy") and [federal](/wiki/Federalism#Federalism_as_the_anarchist_and_libertarian_socialist_mode_of_political_organization "Federalism") or [confederal](/wiki/Confederal "Confederal") associations such as [libertarian municipalism](/wiki/Libertarian_municipalism "Libertarian municipalism"), [citizens' assemblies](/wiki/Citizens%27_assemblies "Citizens' assemblies"), trade unions and [workers' councils](/wiki/Workers%27_council "Workers' council").[[192]](#cite_note-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193)

Anarcho-syndicalist [Gaston Leval](/wiki/Gaston_Leval "Gaston Leval") explained:

> We therefore foresee a Society in which all activities will be coordinated, a structure that has, at the same time, sufficient flexibility to permit the greatest possible autonomy for social life, or for the life of each enterprise, and enough cohesiveness to prevent all disorder. ... In a well-organised society, all of these things must be systematically accomplished by means of parallel federations, vertically united at the highest levels, constituting one vast organism in which all economic functions will be performed in solidarity with all others and that will permanently preserve the necessary cohesion".[[194]](#cite_note-194)

All of this is typically done within a general call for [libertarian](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty")[[195]](#cite_note-195) and [voluntary](/wiki/Voluntaryism "Voluntaryism") [free associations](/wiki/Free_association_(communism_and_anarchism) "Free association (communism and anarchism)")[[196]](#cite_note-196) through the identification, criticism and practical dismantling of illegitimate authority in all aspects of human life.[[197]](#cite_note-auto-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLaughlin20071-93)

As part of the larger socialist movement, it seeks to distinguish itself from Bolshevism, Leninism and Marxism–Leninism as well as social democracy.[[199]](#cite_note-199) Past and present political philosophies and movements commonly described as libertarian socialist include [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") ([anarcho-communism](/wiki/Anarcho-communism "Anarcho-communism"), [anarcho-syndicalism](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalism "Anarcho-syndicalism"),[[200]](#cite_note-200) [collectivist anarchism](/wiki/Collectivist_anarchism "Collectivist anarchism"), [individualist anarchism](/wiki/Individualist_anarchism "Individualist anarchism")[[201]](#cite_note-201)[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203) and [mutualism](/wiki/Mutualism_(economic_theory) "Mutualism (economic theory)")),[[204]](#cite_note-204) [autonomism](/wiki/Autonomism "Autonomism"), [Communalism](/wiki/Communalism_(Bookchin) "Communalism (Bookchin)"), participism, [libertarian Marxism](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism") ([council communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism") and [Luxemburgism](/wiki/Luxemburgism "Luxemburgism")),[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-Graham-2005-206) [revolutionary syndicalism](/wiki/Revolutionary_syndicalism "Revolutionary syndicalism") and [utopian socialism](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism") ([Fourierism](/wiki/Fourierism "Fourierism")).[[207]](#cite_note-207)

### Religious socialism

Main article: [Religious socialism](/wiki/Religious_socialism "Religious socialism")

[Christian socialism](/wiki/Christian_socialism "Christian socialism") is a broad concept involving an intertwining of Christian religion with socialism.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d4/23a-Lam-Alif.png)](/wiki/File:23a-Lam-Alif.png)

Arabic letters "Lam" and "Alif" reading "Lā" (Arabic for "No!") are a symbol of Islamic Socialism in [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey").

[Islamic socialism](/wiki/Islamic_socialism "Islamic socialism") is a more [spiritual](/wiki/Spirituality "Spirituality") form of socialism. Muslim socialists believe that the teachings of the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran") and [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") are not only compatible with, but actively promoting the principles of [equality](/wiki/Social_equality "Social equality") and [public ownership](/wiki/Public_ownership "Public ownership"), drawing inspiration from the early [Medina welfare state](/wiki/Muhammad_in_Medina "Muhammad in Medina") he established. Muslim socialists are more conservative than their Western contemporaries and find their roots in [anti-imperialism](/wiki/Anti-imperialism "Anti-imperialism"), [anti-colonialism](/wiki/Anti-colonialism "Anti-colonialism")[[209]](#cite_note-Reid_1974-209)[[210]](#cite_note-paracha-210) and sometimes, if in an Arab speaking country, [Arab nationalism](/wiki/Arab_nationalism "Arab nationalism"). Islamic socialists believe in deriving legitimacy from political [mandate](/wiki/Mandate_(politics) "Mandate (politics)") as opposed to religious texts.

### Social movements

Main articles: [Eco-socialism](/wiki/Eco-socialism "Eco-socialism"), [Socialism and LGBT rights](/wiki/Socialism_and_LGBT_rights "Socialism and LGBT rights"), and [Socialist feminism](/wiki/Socialist_feminism "Socialist feminism")

See also: [Anarcha-feminism](/wiki/Anarcha-feminism "Anarcha-feminism"), [Anarcho-naturism](/wiki/Anarcho-naturism "Anarcho-naturism"), [Environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism"), [Green anarchism](/wiki/Green_anarchism "Green anarchism"), [Green politics](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics"), [Progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism"), and [Queer anarchism](/wiki/Queer_anarchism "Queer anarchism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Zetkin_luxemburg1910.jpg/250px-Zetkin_luxemburg1910.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zetkin_luxemburg1910.jpg)

Socialist feminist [Clara Zetkin](/wiki/Clara_Zetkin "Clara Zetkin") and Rosa Luxemburg in 1910

Socialist feminism is a branch of [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism") that argues that [liberation](/wiki/Women%27s_liberation "Women's liberation") can only be achieved by working to end both economic and [cultural](/wiki/Cultural "Cultural") sources of women's [oppression](/wiki/Oppression "Oppression").[[211]](#cite_note-211) [Marxist feminism](/wiki/Marxist_feminism "Marxist feminism")'s foundation was laid by Engels in *[The Origin of the Family, Private Property, and the State](/wiki/The_Origin_of_the_Family,_Private_Property,_and_the_State "The Origin of the Family, Private Property, and the State")* (1884). [August Bebel](/wiki/August_Bebel "August Bebel")'s *Woman under Socialism* (1879), is the "single work dealing with sexuality most widely read by rank-and-file members of the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD)".[[212]](#cite_note-212) In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, both [Clara Zetkin](/wiki/Clara_Zetkin "Clara Zetkin") and [Eleanor Marx](/wiki/Eleanor_Marx "Eleanor Marx") were against the [demonisation](/wiki/Demonisation "Demonisation") of men and supported a [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") revolution that would overcome as many male-female inequalities as possible.[[213]](#cite_note-Stokes-213) As their movement already had the most radical demands in women's equality, most Marxist leaders, including Clara Zetkin[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[215]](#cite_note-215) and [Alexandra Kollontai](/wiki/Alexandra_Kollontai "Alexandra Kollontai"),[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217) counterposed Marxism against [liberal feminism](/wiki/Liberal_feminism "Liberal feminism") rather than trying to combine them. Anarcha-feminism began with late 19th- and early 20th-century authors and theorists such as anarchist feminists Goldman and [Voltairine de Cleyre](/wiki/Voltairine_de_Cleyre "Voltairine de Cleyre").[[218]](#cite_note-218) In the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), an anarcha-feminist group, *[Mujeres Libres](/wiki/Mujeres_Libres "Mujeres Libres")* ("Free Women") linked to the *[Federación Anarquista Ibérica](/wiki/Federaci%C3%B3n_Anarquista_Ib%C3%A9rica "Federación Anarquista Ibérica")*, organised to defend both anarchist and feminist ideas.[[219]](#cite_note-219) In 1972, the [Chicago Women's Liberation Union](/wiki/Chicago_Women%27s_Liberation_Union "Chicago Women's Liberation Union") published "Socialist Feminism: A Strategy for the Women's Movement", which is believed to be the first published use of the term "socialist feminism".[[220]](#cite_note-CWLUOver-220)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Day%2C_Fred_Holland_%281864%E2%80%931933%29_-_Edward_Carpenter.jpg/250px-Day%2C_Fred_Holland_%281864%E2%80%931933%29_-_Edward_Carpenter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Day,_Fred_Holland_(1864%E2%80%931933)_-_Edward_Carpenter.jpg)

[Edward Carpenter](/wiki/Edward_Carpenter "Edward Carpenter"), philosopher and activist who was instrumental in the foundation of the [Fabian Society](/wiki/Fabian_Society "Fabian Society") and the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") as well as in the early [LGBTI](/wiki/LGBTI "LGBTI") western movements

Many socialists were early advocates of [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights "LGBT rights"). For early socialist [Charles Fourier](/wiki/Charles_Fourier "Charles Fourier"), true freedom could only occur without suppressing passions, as the suppression of passions is not only destructive to the individual, but to society as a whole. Writing before the advent of the term "homosexuality", Fourier recognised that both men and women have a wide range of sexual needs and preferences which may change throughout their lives, including same-sex sexuality and *androgénité*. He argued that all sexual expressions should be enjoyed as long as people are not abused and that "affirming one's difference" can actually enhance social integration.[[221]](#cite_note-221)[[222]](#cite_note-222) In [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde")'s *[The Soul of Man Under Socialism](/wiki/The_Soul_of_Man_Under_Socialism "The Soul of Man Under Socialism")*, he advocates an [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarian "Egalitarian") society where wealth is shared by all, while warning of the dangers of social systems that crush individuality.[[223]](#cite_note-223) [Edward Carpenter](/wiki/Edward_Carpenter "Edward Carpenter") actively campaigned for homosexual rights. His work *[The Intermediate Sex](/wiki/The_Intermediate_Sex "The Intermediate Sex"): A Study of Some Transitional Types of Men and Women* was a 1908 book arguing for [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation").[[224]](#cite_note-224) who was an influential personality in the foundation of the [Fabian Society](/wiki/Fabian_Society "Fabian Society") and the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). After the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution") under the leadership of Lenin and Trotsky, the Soviet Union abolished previous laws against homosexuality.[[225]](#cite_note-225) [Harry Hay](/wiki/Harry_Hay "Harry Hay") was an early leader in the American LGBT rights movement as well as a member of the [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA"). He is known for his involvement in the founding of gay organisations, including the [Mattachine Society](/wiki/Mattachine_Society "Mattachine Society"), the first sustained gay rights group in the United States which in its early days reflected a strong Marxist influence. The *Encyclopedia of Homosexuality* reports that "[a]s Marxists the founders of the group believed that the injustice and oppression which they suffered stemmed from relationships deeply embedded in the structure of American society".[[226]](#cite_note-226) Emerging from events such as the May 1968 insurrection in France, the [anti-Vietnam war movement](/wiki/Opposition_to_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to the Vietnam War") in the US and the [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") of 1969, militant gay liberation organisations began to spring up around the world. Many sprang from left radicalism more than established homophile groups,[[227]](#cite_note-227) although the [Gay Liberation Front](/wiki/Gay_Liberation_Front "Gay Liberation Front") took an [anti-capitalist](/wiki/Anti-capitalist "Anti-capitalist") stance and attacked the [nuclear family](/wiki/Nuclear_family "Nuclear family") and traditional [gender roles](/wiki/Gender_role "Gender role").[[228]](#cite_note-228)

Eco-socialism is a political strain merging aspects of socialism, Marxism or libertarian socialism with green politics, ecology and [alter-globalisation](/wiki/Alter-globalisation "Alter-globalisation"). Eco-socialists generally claim that the expansion of the capitalist system is the cause of social exclusion, poverty, war and environmental degradation through [globalisation](/wiki/Globalisation "Globalisation") and [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") under the supervision of repressive [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") and transnational structures.[[229]](#cite_note-Manifesto-229) Contrary to the depiction of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") by some environmentalists,[[230]](#cite_note-Eckersley-230) [social ecologists](/wiki/Social_ecology_(theory) "Social ecology (theory)")[[231]](#cite_note-231) and fellow socialists[[232]](#cite_note-Benton-232) as a [productivist](/wiki/Productivism "Productivism") who favoured the domination of nature, eco-socialists revisited Marx's writings and believe that he "was a main originator of the ecological world-view".[[233]](#cite_note-Kovel-233) Marx discussed a "metabolic rift" between man and nature, stating that "private ownership of the globe by single individuals will appear quite absurd as private ownership of one man by another" and his observation that a society must "hand it [the planet] down to succeeding generations in an improved condition".[[234]](#cite_note-Capital3-234) English socialist [William Morris](/wiki/William_Morris "William Morris") is credited with developing principles of what was later called eco-socialism.[[235]](#cite_note-Babylon-235) During the 1880s and 1890s, Morris promoted his ideas within the [Social Democratic Federation](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Federation "Social Democratic Federation") and [Socialist League](/wiki/Socialist_League_(UK,_1885) "Socialist League (UK, 1885)").[[236]](#cite_note-GLSite-236) Green anarchism blends anarchism with [environmental issues](/wiki/Environmental_issues "Environmental issues"). An important early influence was [Henry David Thoreau](/wiki/Henry_David_Thoreau "Henry David Thoreau") and his book *[Walden](/wiki/Walden "Walden")*[[237]](#cite_note-237) as well as [Élisée Reclus](/wiki/%C3%89lis%C3%A9e_Reclus "Élisée Reclus").[[238]](#cite_note-Reclus1-238)[[239]](#cite_note-239)

In the late 19th century, anarcho-naturism fused anarchism and [naturist](/wiki/Naturist "Naturist") philosophies within [individualist anarchist](/wiki/Individualist_anarchist "Individualist anarchist") circles in France, Spain, Cuba[[240]](#cite_note-raforum.info-240) and Portugal.[[241]](#cite_note-spanishind-241) [Murray Bookchin](/wiki/Murray_Bookchin "Murray Bookchin")'s first book *[Our Synthetic Environment](/wiki/Our_Synthetic_Environment "Our Synthetic Environment")*[[242]](#cite_note-242) was followed by his essay "Ecology and Revolutionary Thought" which introduced ecology as a concept in radical politics.[[243]](#cite_note-243) In the 1970s, [Barry Commoner](/wiki/Barry_Commoner "Barry Commoner"), claimed that capitalist technologies were chiefly responsible for [environmental degradation](/wiki/Environmental_degradation "Environmental degradation") as opposed to [population pressures](/wiki/Human_overpopulation "Human overpopulation").[[244]](#cite_note-Commoner-244) In the 1990s socialist/feminists Mary Mellor[[245]](#cite_note-Mellor-245) and [Ariel Salleh](/wiki/Ariel_Salleh "Ariel Salleh")[[246]](#cite_note-Salleh-246) adopt an eco-socialist paradigm. An "environmentalism of the poor" combining ecological awareness and [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") has also become prominent.[[247]](#cite_note-Guha-247) Pepper critiqued the current approach of many within [green politics](/wiki/Green_politics "Green politics"), particularly [deep ecologists](/wiki/Deep_ecologists "Deep ecologists").[[248]](#cite_note-248)

### Syndicalism

Main article: [Syndicalism](/wiki/Syndicalism "Syndicalism")

See also: [Anarcho-syndicalism](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalism "Anarcho-syndicalism")

Syndicalism operates through industrial trade unions. It rejects [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") and the use of establishment politics. Syndicalists reject state power in favour of strategies such as the [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike"). Syndicalists advocate a socialist economy based on federated unions or syndicates of workers who own and manage the means of production. Some Marxist currents advocate syndicalism, such as [De Leonism](/wiki/De_Leonism "De Leonism"). Anarcho-syndicalism views syndicalism as a method for workers in [capitalist society](/wiki/Capitalist_society "Capitalist society") to gain control of an economy.
