The [Spanish Revolution](/wiki/Spanish_Revolution_of_1936 "Spanish Revolution of 1936") was largely orchestrated by the anarcho-syndicalist trade union [CNT](/wiki/Confederaci%C3%B3n_Nacional_del_Trabajo "Confederación Nacional del Trabajo").[[249]](#cite_note-249) The [International Workers' Association](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Association_%E2%80%93_Asociaci%C3%B3n_Internacional_de_los_Trabajadores "International Workers' Association – Asociación Internacional de los Trabajadores") is an international federation of anarcho-syndicalist labour unions and initiatives.[[250]](#cite_note-250)

## Social and political theory

Main article: [Types of socialism](/wiki/Types_of_socialism "Types of socialism")

Early socialist thought took influences from a diverse range of philosophies such as civic [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism "Republicanism"), [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"), [romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), forms of [materialism](/wiki/Materialism "Materialism"), Christianity (both Catholic and Protestant), [natural law](/wiki/Natural_law "Natural law") and [natural rights theory](/wiki/Natural_rights_theory "Natural rights theory"), [utilitarianism](/wiki/Utilitarianism "Utilitarianism") and liberal political economy.[[251]](#cite_note-251) Another philosophical basis for a great deal of early socialism was the emergence of [positivism](/wiki/Positivism "Positivism") during the [European Enlightenment](/wiki/European_Enlightenment "European Enlightenment"). Positivism held that both the natural and social worlds could be understood through scientific knowledge and be analysed using scientific methods.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Claude_Henri_de_Saint-Simon.jpg/250px-Claude_Henri_de_Saint-Simon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claude_Henri_de_Saint-Simon.jpg)

[Claude Henri de Rouvroy, comte de Saint-Simon](/wiki/Henri_de_Saint-Simon "Henri de Saint-Simon"), early French socialist

The fundamental objective of socialism is to attain an advanced level of material production and therefore greater productivity, efficiency and rationality as compared to capitalism and all previous systems, under the view that an expansion of human productive capability is the basis for the extension of freedom and equality in society.[[252]](#cite_note-252) Many forms of socialist theory hold that human behaviour is largely shaped by the social environment. In particular, socialism holds that social [mores](/wiki/Mores "Mores"), values, cultural traits and economic practices are social creations and not the result of an immutable natural law.[[253]](#cite_note-253)[[254]](#cite_note-254) The object of their critique is thus not human avarice or human consciousness, but the material conditions and man-made social systems (i.e. the economic structure of society) which give rise to observed social problems and inefficiencies. [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell"), often considered to be the father of analytic philosophy, identified as a socialist. Russell opposed the class struggle aspects of Marxism, viewing socialism solely as an adjustment of economic relations to accommodate modern machine production to benefit all of humanity through the progressive reduction of necessary work time.[[255]](#cite_note-255)

Socialists view creativity as an essential aspect of human nature and define freedom as a state of being where individuals are able to express their creativity unhindered by constraints of both material scarcity and coercive social institutions.[[256]](#cite_note-256) The socialist concept of individuality is intertwined with the concept of individual creative expression. Karl Marx believed that expansion of the productive forces and technology was the basis for the expansion of human freedom and that socialism, being a system that is consistent with modern developments in technology, would enable the flourishing of "free individualities" through the progressive reduction of necessary labour time. The reduction of necessary labour time to a minimum would grant individuals the opportunity to pursue the development of their true individuality and creativity.[[257]](#cite_note-257)

### Criticism of capitalism

Main articles: [Anti-capitalism](/wiki/Anti-capitalism "Anti-capitalism") and [Criticism of capitalism](/wiki/Criticism_of_capitalism "Criticism of capitalism")

Socialists argue that the accumulation of capital generates waste through [externalities](/wiki/Externality "Externality") that require costly corrective regulatory measures. They also point out that this process generates wasteful industries and practices that exist only to generate sufficient demand for products such as high-pressure advertisement to be sold at a profit, thereby creating rather than satisfying economic demand.[[258]](#cite_note-Use_not_profit-258)[[259]](#cite_note-259) Socialists argue that capitalism consists of irrational activity, such as the purchasing of commodities only to sell at a later time when their price appreciates, rather than for consumption, even if the commodity cannot be sold at a profit to individuals in need and therefore a crucial criticism often made by socialists is that "making money", or accumulation of capital, does not correspond to the satisfaction of demand (the production of [use-values](/wiki/Use-value "Use-value")).[[258]](#cite_note-Use_not_profit-258) The fundamental criterion for economic activity in capitalism is the accumulation of capital for reinvestment in production, but this spurs the development of new, non-productive industries that do not produce use-value and only exist to keep the accumulation process afloat (otherwise the system goes into crisis), such as the spread of the [financial industry](/wiki/Financialization "Financialization"), contributing to the formation of economic bubbles.[[260]](#cite_note-260) Such accumulation and reinvestment, when it demands a constant rate of profit, causes problems if the earnings in the rest of society do not increase in proportion.[[261]](#cite_note-261)

Socialists view [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") relations as limiting the potential of [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces") in the economy. According to socialists, private property becomes obsolete when it concentrates into centralised, socialised institutions not based on private appropriation of revenue*—*but based on cooperative work and internal planning in allocation of inputs—until the role of the capitalist becomes redundant.[[262]](#cite_note-262) With no need for [capital accumulation](/wiki/Capital_accumulation "Capital accumulation") and a class of owners, private property in the means of production is perceived as being an outdated form of economic organisation that should be replaced by a [free association](/wiki/Free_association_(communism_and_anarchism) "Free association (communism and anarchism)") of individuals based on public or [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") of these socialised assets.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorvat198215–201:_Capitalism,_The_General_Pattern_of_Capitalist_Development-263)[[264]](#cite_note-Engels_Selected_Works_1968,_p._40-264) Private ownership imposes constraints on planning, leading to uncoordinated economic decisions that result in business fluctuations, unemployment and a tremendous waste of material resources during crisis of [overproduction](/wiki/Overproduction "Overproduction").[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorvat1982197-265)

Excessive disparities in income distribution lead to social instability and require costly corrective measures in the form of redistributive taxation, which incurs heavy administrative costs while weakening the incentive to work, inviting dishonesty and increasing the likelihood of tax evasion while (the corrective measures) reduce the overall efficiency of the market economy.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorvat1982197–198-266) These corrective policies limit the incentive system of the market by providing things such as [minimum wages](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage"), [unemployment insurance](/wiki/Unemployment_insurance "Unemployment insurance"), taxing profits and reducing the [reserve army of labour](/wiki/Reserve_army_of_labour "Reserve army of labour"), resulting in reduced incentives for capitalists to invest in more production. In essence, social welfare policies cripple capitalism and its incentive system and are thus unsustainable in the long run.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchweickartLawlerTicktinOllman199860–61-267) Marxists argue that the establishment of a [socialist mode of production](/wiki/Socialist_mode_of_production "Socialist mode of production") is the only way to overcome these deficiencies. Socialists and specifically Marxian socialists argue that the inherent conflict of interests between the working class and capital prevent optimal use of available human resources and leads to contradictory interest groups (labour and business) striving to influence the state to intervene in the economy in their favour at the expense of overall economic efficiency. Early socialists ([utopian socialists](/wiki/Utopian_socialists "Utopian socialists") and [Ricardian socialists](/wiki/Ricardian_socialists "Ricardian socialists")) criticised capitalism for concentrating [power](/wiki/Power_(philosophy) "Power (philosophy)") and wealth within a small segment of society.[[268]](#cite_note-268) In addition, they complained that capitalism does not use available technology and resources to their maximum potential in the interests of the public.[[264]](#cite_note-Engels_Selected_Works_1968,_p._40-264)

### Marxism

Main article: [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism")

> At a certain stage of development, the material productive forces of society come into conflict with the existing relations of production or—this merely expresses the same thing in legal terms—with the property relations within the framework of which they have operated hitherto. Then begins an era of social revolution. The changes in the economic foundation lead sooner or later to the transformation of the whole immense superstructure.[[269]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-269)

—Karl Marx, *[Critique of the Gotha Program](/wiki/Critique_of_the_Gotha_Program "Critique of the Gotha Program")*

[Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") argued that socialism would emerge from historical necessity as capitalism rendered itself obsolete and unsustainable from increasing internal contradictions emerging from the development of the [productive forces](/wiki/Productive_forces "Productive forces") and technology. It was these advances in the productive forces combined with the old [social relations of production](/wiki/Social_relations_of_production "Social relations of production") of capitalism that would generate contradictions, leading to working-class consciousness.[[270]](#cite_note-ComparingEconomic-270)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Karl_Marx_by_John_Jabez_Edwin_Mayall_1875_-_Restored.png/250px-Karl_Marx_by_John_Jabez_Edwin_Mayall_1875_-_Restored.png)](/wiki/File:Karl_Marx_by_John_Jabez_Edwin_Mayall_1875_-_Restored.png)

The writings of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") provided the basis for the development of [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") political theory and [Marxian economics](/wiki/Marxian_economics "Marxian economics").

Marx and Engels held the view that the consciousness of those who earn a wage or salary (the working class in the broadest Marxist sense) would be moulded by their conditions of [wage slavery](/wiki/Wage_slavery "Wage slavery"), leading to a tendency to seek their freedom or [emancipation](/wiki/Emancipation_of_labour "Emancipation of labour") by overthrowing ownership of the means of production by capitalists and consequently, overthrowing the state that upheld this economic order. For Marx and Engels, conditions determine consciousness and ending the role of the capitalist class leads eventually to a [classless society](/wiki/Classless_society "Classless society") in which the [state would wither away](/wiki/Withering_away_of_the_state "Withering away of the state").

Marx and Engels used the terms socialism and [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") interchangeably, but many later Marxists defined socialism as a specific historical phase that would displace capitalism and precede communism.[[49]](#cite_note-Steele_1992_44-45-49)[[52]](#cite_note-Hudis-52)

The major characteristics of socialism (particularly as conceived by Marx and Engels after the [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune") of 1871) are that the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") would control the means of production through a [workers' state](/wiki/Workers%27_state "Workers' state") erected by the workers in their interests.

For [orthodox Marxists](/wiki/Orthodox_Marxists "Orthodox Marxists"), socialism is the lower stage of communism based on the principle of "from each according to his ability, [to each according to his contribution](/wiki/To_each_according_to_his_contribution "To each according to his contribution")", while upper stage communism is based on the principle of "[from each according to his ability, to each according to his need](/wiki/From_each_according_to_his_ability,_to_each_according_to_his_need "From each according to his ability, to each according to his need")", the upper stage becoming possible only after the socialist stage further develops economic efficiency and the automation of production has led to a superabundance of goods and services.[[271]](#cite_note-KS-271)[[272]](#cite_note-WA-272) Marx argued that the material productive forces (in industry and commerce) brought into existence by capitalism predicated a cooperative society since production had become a mass social, collective activity of the working class to create commodities but with private ownership (the relations of production or property relations). This conflict between collective effort in large factories and private ownership would bring about a conscious desire in the working class to establish collective ownership commensurate with the collective efforts their daily experience.[[269]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-269)

### Role of the state

See also: [Anti-statism](/wiki/Anti-statism "Anti-statism"), [Marx's theory of the state](/wiki/Marx%27s_theory_of_the_state "Marx's theory of the state"), and [State socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism")

Socialists have taken different perspectives on the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") and the role it should play in revolutionary struggles, in constructing socialism and within an established socialist economy.

In the 19th century, the philosophy of state socialism was first explicitly expounded by the German political philosopher [Ferdinand Lassalle](/wiki/Ferdinand_Lassalle "Ferdinand Lassalle"). In contrast to Karl Marx's perspective of the state, Lassalle rejected the concept of the state as a class-based power structure whose main function was to preserve existing class structures. Lassalle also rejected the Marxist view that the state was destined to "wither away". Lassalle considered the state to be an entity independent of class allegiances and an instrument of justice that would therefore be essential for achieving socialism.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBerlau194921-273)

Preceding the Bolshevik-led revolution in Russia, many socialists including [reformists](/wiki/Reformists "Reformists"), [orthodox Marxist](/wiki/Orthodox_Marxist "Orthodox Marxist") currents such as [council communism](/wiki/Council_communism "Council communism"), anarchists and [libertarian socialists](/wiki/Libertarian_socialists "Libertarian socialists") criticised the idea of using the state to conduct central planning and own the means of production as a way to establish socialism. Following the victory of Leninism in Russia, the idea of "state socialism" spread rapidly throughout the socialist movement and eventually state socialism came to be identified with the [Soviet economic model](/wiki/Soviet_economic_model "Soviet economic model").[[274]](#cite_note-274)

[Joseph Schumpeter](/wiki/Joseph_Schumpeter "Joseph Schumpeter") rejected the association of socialism and social ownership with state ownership over the means of production because the state as it exists in its current form is a product of capitalist society and cannot be transplanted to a different institutional framework. Schumpeter argued that there would be different institutions within socialism than those that exist within modern capitalism, just as [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") had its own distinct and unique institutional forms. The state, along with concepts like [property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") and taxation, were concepts exclusive to commercial society (capitalism) and attempting to place them within the context of a future socialist society would amount to a distortion of these concepts by using them out of context.[[275]](#cite_note-275)

### Utopian versus scientific

Main articles: [Scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism") and [Utopian socialism](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism")

Utopian socialism is a term used to define the first currents of modern socialist thought as exemplified by the work of [Henri de Saint-Simon](/wiki/Henri_de_Saint-Simon "Henri de Saint-Simon"), [Charles Fourier](/wiki/Charles_Fourier "Charles Fourier") and [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen") which inspired [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and other early socialists.[[276]](#cite_note-276) Visions of imaginary ideal societies, which competed with revolutionary social democratic movements, were viewed as not being grounded in the material conditions of society and as reactionary.[[277]](#cite_note-Draper-277) Although it is technically possible for any set of ideas or any person living at any time in history to be a utopian socialist, the term is most often applied to those socialists who lived in the first quarter of the 19th century who were ascribed the label "utopian" by later socialists as a negative term to imply naivete and dismiss their ideas as fanciful or unrealistic.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman2005-102)

Religious sects whose members live communally such as the [Hutterites](/wiki/Hutterites "Hutterites") are not usually called "utopian socialists", although their way of living is a prime example. They have been categorised as [religious socialists](/wiki/Religious_socialists "Religious socialists") by some. Similarly, modern [intentional communities](/wiki/Intentional_communities "Intentional communities") based on socialist ideas could also be categorised as "utopian socialist". For Marxists, the development of capitalism in Western Europe provided a material basis for the possibility of bringing about socialism because according to *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* "[w]hat the bourgeoisie produces above all is its own grave diggers",[[278]](#cite_note-278) namely the working class, which must become conscious of the historical objectives set it by society.

### Reform versus revolution

Main articles: [Reformism](/wiki/Reformism "Reformism") and [Revolutionary socialism](/wiki/Revolutionary_socialism "Revolutionary socialism")

Revolutionary socialists believe that a social revolution is necessary to effect structural changes to the socioeconomic structure of society. Among revolutionary socialists there are differences in strategy, theory and the definition of *revolution*. [Orthodox Marxists](/wiki/Orthodox_Marxists "Orthodox Marxists") and [left communists](/wiki/Left_communists "Left communists") take an [impossibilist](/wiki/Impossibilist "Impossibilist") stance, believing that revolution should be spontaneous as a result of contradictions in society due to technological changes in the productive forces. Lenin theorised that under capitalism the workers cannot achieve class consciousness beyond organising into trade unions and making demands of the capitalists. Therefore, [Leninists](/wiki/Leninists "Leninists") argue that it is historically necessary for a [vanguard](/wiki/Vanguardism "Vanguardism") of class conscious revolutionaries to take a central role in coordinating the social revolution to overthrow the capitalist state and eventually the institution of the state altogether.[[279]](#cite_note-279) *Revolution* is not necessarily defined by revolutionary socialists as violent insurrection,[[280]](#cite_note-280) but as a complete dismantling and rapid transformation of all areas of class society led by the majority of the masses: the working class.

Reformism is generally associated with [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") and [gradualist](/wiki/Gradualist "Gradualist") [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism"). Reformism is the belief that socialists should stand in parliamentary elections within capitalist society and if elected use the [machinery of government](/wiki/Machinery_of_government "Machinery of government") to pass political and social reforms for the purposes of ameliorating the instabilities and inequities of capitalism. Within socialism, *reformism* is used in two different ways. One has no intention of bringing about socialism or fundamental economic change to society and is used to oppose such structural changes. The other is based on the assumption that while reforms are not socialist in themselves, they can help rally supporters to the cause of revolution by popularizing the cause of socialism to the working class.[[281]](#cite_note-Parker-281)

The debate on the ability for social democratic reformism to lead to a socialist transformation of society is over a century old. Reformism is criticized for being paradoxical as it seeks to overcome the existing economic system of capitalism while trying to improve the conditions of capitalism, thereby making it appear more tolerable to society. According to [Rosa Luxemburg](/wiki/Rosa_Luxemburg "Rosa Luxemburg"), capitalism is not overthrown, "but is on the contrary strengthened by the development of social reforms".[[282]](#cite_note-282) In a similar vein, Stan Parker of the [Socialist Party of Great Britain](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_Great_Britain "Socialist Party of Great Britain") argues that reforms are a diversion of energy for socialists and are limited because they must adhere to the logic of capitalism.[[281]](#cite_note-Parker-281) French social theorist [André Gorz](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Gorz "André Gorz") criticized reformism by advocating a third alternative to reformism and social revolution that he called "[non-reformist reforms](/wiki/Non-reformist_reforms "Non-reformist reforms")", specifically focused on structural changes to capitalism as opposed to reforms to improve living conditions within capitalism or to prop it up through economic interventions.[[283]](#cite_note-283)

## Culture

See also: [Proletarian literature](/wiki/Proletarian_literature "Proletarian literature"), [Counterculture](/wiki/Counterculture "Counterculture"), [New York Intellectuals](/wiki/New_York_Intellectuals "New York Intellectuals"), [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left"), [Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), [Russian Futurism](/wiki/Russian_Futurism "Russian Futurism"), [Constructivism (art)](/wiki/Constructivism_(art) "Constructivism (art)"), [Constructivist architecture](/wiki/Constructivist_architecture "Constructivist architecture"), and [Russian avant-garde](/wiki/Russian_avant-garde "Russian avant-garde")

> Under Socialism, solidarity will be the basis of society. Literature and art will be tuned to a different key.

—Trotsky, *Literature and Revolution*, 1924[[284]](#cite_note-284)[[285]](#cite_note-285)

In the Leninist conception, the role of the vanguard party was to politically educate the workers and peasants to dispel the societal [false consciousness](/wiki/False_consciousness "False consciousness") of institutional religion and nationalism that constitute the [cultural *status quo*](/wiki/Cultural_hegemony "Cultural hegemony") taught by the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") to the proletariat to facilitate their economic [exploitation](/wiki/Exploitation_of_labour "Exploitation of labour") of peasants and workers. Influenced by Lenin, the [Central Committee of the Bolshevik Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Central Committee of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") stated that the development of the socialist workers' culture should not be "hamstrung from above" and opposed the *[Proletkult](/wiki/Proletkult "Proletkult")* (1917–1925) organisational control of the national culture.[[286]](#cite_note-286) Similarly, Trotsky viewed the party as transmitters of culture to the masses for raising the standards of [education](/wiki/Education "Education"), as well as entry into the cultural sphere, but that the process of artistic creation in terms of language and presentation should be the domain of the practitioner. According to political scientist Baruch Knei-Paz in his book *[The Social and Political Thought of Leon Trotsky](/wiki/The_Social_and_Political_Thought_of_Leon_Trotsky "The Social and Political Thought of Leon Trotsky")*, this represented one of several distinctions between Trotsky's approach on cultural matters and [Stalin's policy in the 1930s](/wiki/Socialist_realism#Stalin_era "Socialist realism").[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnei-Paz1978[httpsarchiveorgdetailssocialpoliticalt0000kneipage300mode2upqproletarianculture_289–301]-287)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Mural._El_hombre_en_el_cruce_de_caminos._Diego_rivera_%281934%29.JPG/500px-Mural._El_hombre_en_el_cruce_de_caminos._Diego_rivera_%281934%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mural._El_hombre_en_el_cruce_de_caminos._Diego_rivera_(1934).JPG)

[Man, Controller of the Universe](/wiki/Man_at_the_Crossroads "Man at the Crossroads") produced by Mexican artist [Diego Rivera](/wiki/Diego_Rivera "Diego Rivera")

In *[Literature and Revolution](/wiki/Literature_and_Revolution "Literature and Revolution")*, Trotsky examined aesthetic issues in relation to class and the Russian revolution. Soviet scholar Robert Bird considered his work as the "first systematic treatment of art by a Communist leader" and a catalyst for later, Marxist cultural and critical theories.[[288]](#cite_note-288)

In *"[Problems of Everyday Life](/wiki/Problems_of_Everyday_Life "Problems of Everyday Life")"*, a contemporaneous book which further articulated his views on culture and science, Trotsky argued that cultural development was a prerequisite for socialist reconstruction.[[289]](#cite_note-289) In particular, he argued that cultural development would accentuate industrial and technical progress. He viewed both elements to be interrelated components as part of dialectical interaction in which he viewed the low level of Russian [technique](/wiki/Technology "Technology") and [expertise](/wiki/Expertise "Expertise") to be a function of cultural backwardness. According to Trotsky, Western industrial techniques and products such as the [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio") should not be rejected due to their status as a product of a capitalist system but rather absorbed into the Soviet socialist framework to facilitate new forms of techniques and cultural production.[[290]](#cite_note-Knei-Paz_282-290) In this interpretation, the [transference of techniques](/wiki/Technology_transfer "Technology transfer") brought new cultural changes in terms of rationalism, [efficiency](/wiki/Efficiency "Efficiency"), exactitude and [quality](/wiki/Service_quality "Service quality").[[290]](#cite_note-Knei-Paz_282-290)

Trotsky would later co-author the 1938 *[Manifesto for an Independent Revolutionary Art](/wiki/Manifesto_for_an_Independent_Revolutionary_Art "Manifesto for an Independent Revolutionary Art")* with the endorsement of prominent artists [Andre Breton](/wiki/Andre_Breton "Andre Breton") and [Diego Rivera](/wiki/Diego_Rivera "Diego Rivera").[[291]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeutscher20151474–1475-291) Trotsky's writings on literature such as his 1923 survey which advocated tolerance, limited censorship and respect for literary tradition had strong appeal to the [New York Intellectuals](/wiki/New_York_Intellectuals "New York Intellectuals").[[292]](#cite_note-292)

Prior to Stalin's rule, [literary, religious and national representatives](/wiki/Russian_literature#Early_post-Revolutionary_era "Russian literature") had some level of autonomy in [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") throughout the 1920s but these groups were later rigorously repressed during the [Stalinist era](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism"). [Socialist realism](/wiki/Socialist_realism "Socialist realism") was imposed under Stalin in artistic production and other creative industries such as [music](/wiki/Music "Music"), [film](/wiki/Film "Film") along with [sports](/wiki/Sports "Sports") were subject to extreme levels of political control.[[293]](#cite_note-293)

The [counter-cultural](/wiki/Counter-cultural "Counter-cultural") phenomenon which emerged in the 1960s shaped the [intellectual](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual") and radical outlook of the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left"); this movement placed a heavy emphasis on [anti-racism](/wiki/Anti-racism#Anti-racism_and_socialism "Anti-racism"), [anti-imperialism](/wiki/Anti-imperialism "Anti-imperialism") and [direct democracy](/wiki/Direct_democracy "Direct democracy") in opposition to the [dominant culture](/wiki/Culture_of_capitalism "Culture of capitalism") of advanced [industrial capitalism](/wiki/Industrial_capitalism "Industrial capitalism").[[294]](#cite_note-294)
Socialist groups have also been closely involved with a number of counter-cultural movements such as [Vietnam Solidarity Campaign](/wiki/Vietnam_Solidarity_Campaign "Vietnam Solidarity Campaign"), [Stop the War Coalition](/wiki/Stop_the_War_Coalition "Stop the War Coalition"), [Love Music Hate Racism](/wiki/Love_Music_Hate_Racism "Love Music Hate Racism"), [Anti-Nazi League](/wiki/Anti-Nazi_League "Anti-Nazi League")[[295]](#cite_note-295) and [Unite Against Fascism](/wiki/Unite_Against_Fascism "Unite Against Fascism").[[296]](#cite_note-296)

Main article: [Socialist economics](/wiki/Socialist_economics "Socialist economics")

See also: [Production for use](/wiki/Production_for_use "Production for use")

> The economic anarchy of capitalist society as it exists today is, in my opinion, the real source of the evil. ... I am convinced there is only one way to eliminate these grave evils, namely through the establishment of a socialist economy, accompanied by an educational system which would be oriented toward social goals. In such an economy, the means of production are owned by society itself and are utilised in a planned fashion. A planned economy, which adjusts production to the needs of the community, would distribute the work to be done among all those able to work and would guarantee a livelihood to every man, woman, and child. The education of the individual, in addition to promoting his own innate abilities, would attempt to develop in him a sense of responsibility for his fellow men in place of the glorification of power and success in our present society.[[297]](#cite_note-297)

—[Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein"), "[Why Socialism?](/wiki/Why_Socialism%3F "Why Socialism?")", 1949

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Albert_Einstein_Head.jpg/250px-Albert_Einstein_Head.jpg)](/wiki/File:Albert_Einstein_Head.jpg)

Albert Einstein advocated for a socialist [planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") with his 1949 article "[Why Socialism?](/wiki/Why_Socialism%3F "Why Socialism?")"

Socialist economics starts from the premise that "individuals do not live or work in isolation but live in cooperation with one another. Furthermore, everything that people produce is in some sense a social product, and everyone who contributes to the production of a good is entitled to a share in it. Society as whole, therefore, should own or at least control property for the benefit of all its members".[[298]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-298)

The original conception of socialism was an economic system whereby production was organised in a way to directly produce goods and services for their utility (or use-value in [classical](/wiki/Classical_economics "Classical economics") and [Marxian economics](/wiki/Marxian_economics "Marxian economics")), with the direct allocation of resources in terms of physical units as opposed to financial calculation and the economic laws of capitalism (see [law of value](/wiki/Law_of_value "Law of value")), often entailing the end of capitalistic economic categories such as [rent](/wiki/Renting "Renting"), interest, [profit](/wiki/Profit_(economics) "Profit (economics)") and money.[[299]](#cite_note-299) In a fully developed socialist economy, production and balancing factor inputs with outputs becomes a technical process to be undertaken by engineers.[[300]](#cite_note-300)

[Market socialism](/wiki/Market_socialism "Market socialism") refers to an array of different economic theories and systems that use the market mechanism to organise production and to allocate factor inputs among socially owned enterprises, with the economic surplus (profits) accruing to society in a [social dividend](/wiki/Social_dividend "Social dividend") as opposed to private capital owners.[[301]](#cite_note-301) Variations of market socialism include [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian") proposals such as [mutualism](/wiki/Mutualism_(economic_theory) "Mutualism (economic theory)"), based on classical economics, and [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_economics "Neoclassical economics") economic models such as the [Lange model](/wiki/Lange_model "Lange model"). Some economists, such as [Joseph Stiglitz](/wiki/Joseph_Stiglitz "Joseph Stiglitz"), [Mancur Olson](/wiki/Mancur_Olson "Mancur Olson"), and others not specifically advancing anti-socialists positions have shown that prevailing economic models upon which such democratic or market socialism models might be based have logical flaws or unworkable presuppositions.[[302]](#cite_note-302)[[303]](#cite_note-303) These criticisms have been incorporated into the models of market socialism developed by [John Roemer](/wiki/John_Roemer "John Roemer") and [Nicholas Vrousalis](/w/index.php?title=Nicholas_Vrousalis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nicholas Vrousalis (page does not exist)").[[304]](#cite_note-304)[[305]](#cite_note-305)[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

The ownership of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") can be based on direct ownership by the users of the productive property through [worker cooperative](/wiki/Worker_cooperative "Worker cooperative"); or [commonly owned](/wiki/Commonly_owned "Commonly owned") by all of society with management and control delegated to those who operate/use the means of production; or [public ownership](/wiki/Public_ownership "Public ownership") by a state apparatus. Public ownership may refer to the creation of [state-owned enterprises](/wiki/State-owned_enterprises "State-owned enterprises"), [nationalisation](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation"), [municipalisation](/wiki/Municipalisation "Municipalisation") or autonomous collective institutions. Some socialists feel that in a socialist economy, at least the "[commanding heights](/wiki/Commanding_Heights:_The_Battle_for_the_World_Economy "Commanding Heights: The Battle for the World Economy")" of the economy must be publicly owned.[[306]](#cite_note-306) [Economic liberals](/wiki/Economic_liberals "Economic liberals") and [right libertarians](/wiki/Right_libertarians "Right libertarians") view private ownership of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") and the market exchange as natural entities or moral rights which are central to their conceptions of freedom and liberty and view the economic dynamics of capitalism as immutable and absolute, therefore they perceive public ownership of the means of production, [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperatives "Cooperatives") and [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning") as infringements upon liberty.[[307]](#cite_note-milton-307)[[308]](#cite_note-308)

Management and control over the activities of enterprises are based on self-management and self-governance, with equal power-relations in the workplace to maximise occupational autonomy. A socialist form of organisation would eliminate controlling hierarchies so that only a hierarchy based on technical knowledge in the workplace remains. Every member would have decision-making power in the firm and would be able to participate in establishing its overall policy objectives. The policies/goals would be carried out by the technical specialists that form the coordinating hierarchy of the firm, who would establish plans or directives for the work community to accomplish these goals.[[309]](#cite_note-The_Political_Economy_of_Socialism,_1982._p._197-309)[[310]](#cite_note-310)

The role and use of money in a hypothetical socialist economy is a contested issue. Nineteenth century socialists including [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen"), [Pierre-Joseph Proudhon](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph_Proudhon "Pierre-Joseph Proudhon") and [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill") advocated various forms of [labour vouchers](/wiki/Labour_voucher "Labour voucher") or labour credits, which like money would be used to acquire articles of consumption, but unlike money they are unable to become [capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital") and would not be used to allocate resources within the production process. Bolshevik revolutionary [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") argued that money could not be arbitrarily abolished following a socialist revolution. Money had to exhaust its "historic mission", meaning it would have to be used until its function became redundant, eventually being transformed into bookkeeping receipts for statisticians and only in the more distant future would money not be required for even that role.[[311]](#cite_note-311)

### Planned economy

Main article: [Planned economy](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy")

A planned economy is a type of economy consisting of a mixture of public ownership of the means of production and the coordination of production and distribution through [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning"). A planned economy can be either decentralised or centralised. [Enrico Barone](/wiki/Enrico_Barone "Enrico Barone") provided a comprehensive theoretical framework for a planned socialist economy. In his model, assuming perfect computation techniques, simultaneous equations relating inputs and outputs to ratios of equivalence would provide appropriate valuations to balance supply and demand.[[312]](#cite_note-312)

The most prominent example of a planned economy was the [economic system of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Soviet_Union "Economy of the Soviet Union") and as such the centralised-planned economic model is usually associated with the [communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") of the 20th century, where it was combined with a single-party political system. In a centrally planned economy, decisions regarding the quantity of goods and services to be produced are planned in advance by a planning agency (see also the [analysis of Soviet-type economic planning](/wiki/Analysis_of_Soviet-type_economic_planning "Analysis of Soviet-type economic planning")). The economic systems of the Soviet Union and the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") are further classified as "command economies", which are defined as systems where economic coordination is undertaken by commands, directives and production targets.[[313]](#cite_note-313) Studies by economists of various political persuasions on the actual functioning of the Soviet economy indicate that it was not actually a planned economy. Instead of conscious planning, the Soviet economy was based on a process whereby the plan was modified by localised agents and the original plans went largely unfulfilled. Planning agencies, ministries and enterprises all adapted and bargained with each other during the formulation of the plan as opposed to following a plan passed down from a higher authority, leading some economists to suggest that planning did not actually take place within the Soviet economy and that a better description would be an "administered" or "managed" economy.[[314]](#cite_note-314)

Although central planning was largely supported by [Marxist–Leninists](/wiki/Marxist%E2%80%93Leninists "Marxist–Leninists"), some factions within the Soviet Union before the rise of [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") held positions contrary to central planning. Leon Trotsky rejected central planning in favour of decentralised planning. He argued that central planners, regardless of their intellectual capacity, would be unable to coordinate effectively all economic activity within an economy because they operated without the input and tacit knowledge embodied by the participation of the millions of people in the economy. As a result, central planners would be unable to respond to local economic conditions.[[315]](#cite_note-315)

### Self-managed economy

Main articles: [Decentralised planning](/wiki/Decentralised_planning "Decentralised planning"), [Economic democracy](/wiki/Economic_democracy "Economic democracy"), [Workers' self-management](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management"), and [Socialist self-management](/wiki/Socialist_self-management "Socialist self-management")

> Socialism, you see, is a bird with two wings. The definition is 'social ownership and democratic control of the instruments and means of production.'[[316]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESinclair1918-316)

—[Upton Sinclair](/wiki/Upton_Sinclair "Upton Sinclair")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Lev_Trotsky_1906-3.3_V1.jpg/250px-Lev_Trotsky_1906-3.3_V1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lev_Trotsky_1906-3.3_V1.jpg)

The [Soviet of Workers' Deputies of St. Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Soviet "Saint Petersburg Soviet") in 1905, Trotsky in the center. The [soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") were an early example of a [workers council](/wiki/Workers_council "Workers council").

A self-managed, decentralised economy is based on autonomous self-regulating economic units and a decentralised mechanism of resource allocation and decision-making. This model has found support in notable classical and neoclassical economists including [Alfred Marshall](/wiki/Alfred_Marshall "Alfred Marshall"), [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill") and [Jaroslav Vanek](/wiki/Jaroslav_Vanek "Jaroslav Vanek"). There are numerous variations of self-management, including labour-managed firms and worker-managed firms. The goals of self-management are to eliminate exploitation and reduce [alienation](/wiki/Social_alienation "Social alienation").[[317]](#cite_note-317) [Guild socialism](/wiki/Guild_socialism "Guild socialism") is a political movement advocating [workers' control](/wiki/Workers%27_control "Workers' control") of industry through the medium of [trade-related guilds](/wiki/Trade-related_guilds "Trade-related guilds") "in an implied contractual relationship with the public".[[318]](#cite_note-britannica.com-318) It originated in the United Kingdom and was at its most influential in the first quarter of the 20th century.[[318]](#cite_note-britannica.com-318) It was strongly associated with [G. D. H. Cole](/wiki/G._D._H._Cole "G. D. H. Cole") and influenced by the ideas of [William Morris](/wiki/William_Morris "William Morris").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/CyberSyn-render-106.png/250px-CyberSyn-render-106.png)](/wiki/File:CyberSyn-render-106.png)

[Project Cybersyn](/wiki/Project_Cybersyn "Project Cybersyn") was an early form of computational [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning")

One such system is the cooperative economy, a largely free [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") in which workers manage the firms and democratically determine remuneration levels and labour divisions. Productive resources would be legally owned by the [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative") and rented to the workers, who would enjoy [usufruct](/wiki/Usufruct "Usufruct") rights.[[319]](#cite_note-319) Another form of decentralised planning is the use of [cybernetics](/wiki/Cybernetics "Cybernetics"), or the use of computers to manage the allocation of economic inputs. The socialist-run government of [Salvador Allende](/wiki/Salvador_Allende "Salvador Allende") in Chile experimented with [Project Cybersyn](/wiki/Project_Cybersyn "Project Cybersyn"), a real-time information bridge between the government, state enterprises and consumers.[[320]](#cite_note-320) This had been preceded by similar efforts to introduce a form of cybernetic economic planning as seen with the proposed [OGAS](/wiki/OGAS "OGAS") system in the Soviet Union. The OGAS project was conceived to oversee a nationwide [information network](/wiki/Information_network "Information network") but was never implemented due to conflicting, [bureaucratic interests](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura").[[321]](#cite_note-321) Another, more recent variant is [participatory economics](/wiki/Participatory_economics "Participatory economics"), wherein the economy is planned by decentralised councils of workers and consumers. Workers would be remunerated solely according to effort and sacrifice, so that those engaged in dangerous, uncomfortable and strenuous work would receive the highest incomes and could thereby work less.[[322]](#cite_note-322) A contemporary model for a self-managed, non-market socialism is [Pat Devine](/wiki/Pat_Devine "Pat Devine")'s model of negotiated coordination. Negotiated coordination is based upon social ownership by those affected by the use of the assets involved, with decisions made by those at the most localised level of production.[[323]](#cite_note-323)

[Michel Bauwens](/wiki/Michel_Bauwens "Michel Bauwens") identifies the emergence of the open software movement and [peer-to-peer production](/wiki/Social_peer-to-peer_processes "Social peer-to-peer processes") as a new alternative [mode of production](/wiki/Mode_of_production "Mode of production") to the capitalist economy and centrally planned economy that is based on collaborative self-management, common ownership of resources and the production of use-values through the free cooperation of producers who have access to distributed capital.[[324]](#cite_note-CTheory-324)

[Anarcho-communism](/wiki/Anarcho-communism "Anarcho-communism") is a theory of [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") which advocates the abolition of the [state](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), [private property](/wiki/Private_property "Private property") and capitalism in favour of [common ownership](/wiki/Common_ownership "Common ownership") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production").[[325]](#cite_note-Mayne-325)[[326]](#cite_note-326) [Anarcho-syndicalism](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalism "Anarcho-syndicalism") was practised in [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") and other places in the [Spanish Revolution](/wiki/Spanish_Revolution_of_1936 "Spanish Revolution of 1936") during the Spanish Civil War. [Sam Dolgoff](/wiki/Sam_Dolgoff "Sam Dolgoff") estimated that about eight million people participated directly or at least indirectly in the Spanish Revolution.[[327]](#cite_note-Dolgoff1974-327)

The economy of the former [Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") established a [system](/wiki/Socialist_self-management "Socialist self-management") based on market-based allocation, social ownership of the means of production and self-management within firms. This system substituted Yugoslavia's Soviet-type central planning with a decentralised, self-managed system after reforms in 1953.[[328]](#cite_note-328)

The [Marxian economist](/wiki/Marxian_economist "Marxian economist") [Richard D. Wolff](/wiki/Richard_D._Wolff "Richard D. Wolff") argues that "re-organising production so that workers become collectively self-directed at their work-sites" not only moves society beyond both capitalism and [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") of the last century, but would also mark another milestone in human history, similar to earlier transitions out of slavery and feudalism.[[329]](#cite_note-329) As an example, Wolff claims that [Mondragon](/wiki/Mondragon_Corporation "Mondragon Corporation") is "a stunningly successful alternative to the capitalist organisation of production".[[330]](#cite_note-330)

### State-directed economy

Main article: [State socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism")

See also: [Dirigisme](/wiki/Dirigisme "Dirigisme"), [Nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization"), [Public ownership](/wiki/Public_ownership "Public ownership"), [State capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism"), and [Steady-state economy](/wiki/Steady-state_economy "Steady-state economy")

State socialism can be used to classify any variety of socialist philosophies that advocates the ownership of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") by the [state apparatus](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), either as a transitional stage between capitalism and socialism, or as an end-goal in itself. Typically, it refers to a form of technocratic management, whereby technical specialists administer or manage economic enterprises on behalf of society and the public interest instead of workers' councils or workplace democracy.

A state-directed economy may refer to a type of mixed economy consisting of public ownership over large industries, as promoted by various Social democratic political parties during the 20th century. This ideology influenced the policies of the British Labour Party during Clement Attlee's administration. In the biography of the 1945 United Kingdom Labour Party Prime Minister [Clement Attlee](/wiki/Clement_Attlee "Clement Attlee"), [Francis Beckett](/wiki/Francis_Beckett "Francis Beckett") states: "[T]he government ... wanted what would become known as a mixed economy."[[331]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckett2007-331)

Nationalisation in the United Kingdom was achieved through compulsory purchase of the industry (i.e. with compensation). [British Aerospace](/wiki/British_Aerospace "British Aerospace") was a combination of major aircraft companies [British Aircraft Corporation](/wiki/British_Aircraft_Corporation "British Aircraft Corporation"), [Hawker Siddeley](/wiki/Hawker_Siddeley "Hawker Siddeley") and others. [British Shipbuilders](/wiki/British_Shipbuilders "British Shipbuilders") was a combination of the major shipbuilding companies including [Cammell Laird](/wiki/Cammell_Laird "Cammell Laird"), [Govan Shipbuilders](/wiki/Govan_Shipbuilders "Govan Shipbuilders"), [Swan Hunter](/wiki/Swan_Hunter "Swan Hunter") and [Yarrow Shipbuilders](/wiki/Yarrow_Shipbuilders "Yarrow Shipbuilders"), whereas the nationalisation of the coal mines in 1947 created a coal board charged with running the coal industry commercially so as to be able to meet the interest payable on the bonds which the former mine owners' shares had been converted into.[[332]](#cite_note-332)[[333]](#cite_note-333)

### Market socialism

Main article: [Market socialism](/wiki/Market_socialism "Market socialism")

Market socialism consists of publicly owned or cooperatively owned enterprises operating in a [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy"). It is a system that uses the market and [monetary prices](/wiki/Price_system "Price system") for the allocation and accounting of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), thereby retaining the process of [capital accumulation](/wiki/Capital_accumulation "Capital accumulation"). The profit generated would be used to directly remunerate employees, collectively sustain the enterprise or finance public institutions.[[334]](#cite_note-334) In state-oriented forms of market socialism, in which state enterprises attempt to maximise profit, the profits can be used to fund government programs and services through a [social dividend](/wiki/Social_dividend "Social dividend"), eliminating or greatly diminishing the need for various forms of taxation that exist in capitalist systems. [Neoclassical economist](/wiki/Neoclassical_economist "Neoclassical economist") [Léon Walras](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Walras "Léon Walras") believed that a socialist economy based on state ownership of land and natural resources would provide a means of public finance to make income taxes unnecessary.[[335]](#cite_note-335) [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") implemented a market socialist economy based on cooperatives and worker self-management.[[336]](#cite_note-336) Some of the economic reforms introduced during the [Prague Spring](/wiki/Prague_Spring "Prague Spring") by [Alexander Dubček](/wiki/Alexander_Dub%C4%8Dek "Alexander Dubček"), the [leader](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Czechoslovakia#Leaders "Communist Party of Czechoslovakia") of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), included elements of market socialism.[[337]](#cite_note-337)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Proudhon-children.jpg/250px-Proudhon-children.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proudhon-children.jpg)

[Pierre-Joseph Proudhon](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph_Proudhon "Pierre-Joseph Proudhon"), main theorist of [mutualism](/wiki/Mutualism_(economic_theory) "Mutualism (economic theory)") and influential French socialist thinker

[Mutualism](/wiki/Mutualism_(economy) "Mutualism (economy)") is an [economic theory](/wiki/Economics "Economics") and [anarchist school of thought](/wiki/Anarchist_school_of_thought "Anarchist school of thought") that advocates a society where each person might possess a [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production"), either individually or collectively, with trade representing equivalent amounts of labour in the [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market").[[338]](#cite_note-338) Integral to the scheme was the establishment of a mutual-credit bank that would lend to producers at a minimal interest rate, just high enough to cover administration.[[339]](#cite_note-339) Mutualism is based on a [labour theory of value](/wiki/Labour_theory_of_value "Labour theory of value") that holds that when labour or its product is sold, in exchange it ought to receive goods or services embodying "the amount of labour necessary to produce an article of exactly similar and equal utility".[[340]](#cite_note-340)

The current economic system in China is formally referred to as a [socialist market economy with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialist_market_economy_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialist market economy with Chinese characteristics"). It combines a large state sector that comprises the commanding heights of the economy, which are guaranteed their public ownership status by law,[[341]](#cite_note-341) with a [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") mainly engaged in commodity production and light industry responsible from anywhere between 33%[[342]](#cite_note-342) to over 70% of GDP generated in 2005.[[343]](#cite_note-343) The current Chinese economy consists of 150 [corporatised](/wiki/Corporatised "Corporatised") state-owned enterprises that report directly to China's central government.[[344]](#cite_note-344) By 2008, these state-owned corporations had become increasingly dynamic and generated large increases in revenue for the state,[[345]](#cite_note-345)[[346]](#cite_note-346) resulting in a state-sector led recovery during the 2009 financial crises while accounting for most of China's economic growth.[[347]](#cite_note-347) The Chinese economic model is widely cited as a contemporary form of state capitalism, the major difference between Western capitalism and the Chinese model being the degree of state-ownership of shares in publicly listed corporations. The Socialist Republic of Vietnam has adopted a similar model after the [Doi Moi](/wiki/Doi_Moi "Doi Moi") economic renovation but slightly differs from the Chinese model in that the Vietnamese government retains firm control over the state sector and strategic industries, but allows for private-sector activity in commodity production.[[348]](#cite_note-348)

## Analysis of the Soviet Model

#### Achievements of the Soviet planned economies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Sputnik-stamp-ussr.jpg/250px-Sputnik-stamp-ussr.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sputnik-stamp-ussr.jpg)

Soviet stamp showing the orbit of [Sputnik 1](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1")

In the officially sanctioned textbooks describing the socialist planned economies as they existed in the 1980s, it was claimed as follows:

* Class and national oppression had been totally eradicated.
* [Unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment"), [hunger](/wiki/Hunger "Hunger"), [poverty](/wiki/Poverty "Poverty"), [illiteracy](/wiki/Illiteracy "Illiteracy") and uncertainty about the future had been eliminated.
* Every citizen had a guaranteed right to work, rest, [education](/wiki/Universal_education "Universal education"), [health care](/wiki/Universal_healthcare "Universal healthcare"), abode and security in old age and maintenance in the event of disability.
* Material standards of living were rising steadily and everyone had free access to knowledge and to the values of world and national culture.
* Every citizen had a right in practice to take part in discussing and solving any problems in the life of the enterprise, region, republic and the country they lived in, including the rights to free speech, of assembly and to demonstrate.[[349]](#cite_note-349)

Data collected by the United Nations of indicators of human development in the early 1990s show that a high level of social development was achieved in the former socialist planned economies of Central and Eastern Europe and the Commonwealth of Independent States (CEE/CIS). Life expectancy in the CEE/CIS area in the period 1985–1990 was 68 years, while for the countries of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) it was 75 years.[[350]](#cite_note-350) Infant mortality in the CEE/CIS area was 25 for every 1,000 live births in 1990, compared to 13 in the OECD area.[[351]](#cite_note-351) In terms of education, the two areas enjoyed universal adult literacy and full enrolment of children in primary and secondary schools. For tertiary education, the CEE/CIS had 2,600 university students per 100,000 population, while in the OECD the comparable figure was 3,550 students. Overall enrolment at primary, secondary and tertiary levels was 75 percent in the CEE/CIS region and 82 percent in the OECD countries.[[352]](#cite_note-352)

The Soviet Union placed great emphasis on [science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_the_Soviet_Union "Science and technology in the Soviet Union").[[353]](#cite_note-353)[[354]](#cite_note-354) Lenin believed the USSR would never overtake the developed world if it remained as technologically backward as it was upon its founding. Soviet authorities proved their commitment to Lenin's belief by developing massive networks and research and development organizations. In the early 1960s, 40% of chemistry PhDs in the Soviet Union were attained by women, compared with only 5% in the United States.[[355]](#cite_note-355) By 1989, Soviet scientists were among the world's best-trained specialists in several areas, such as energy physics, selected areas of medicine, mathematics, welding, space technology, and military technologies. However, due to rigid state planning and [bureaucracy](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura"), the Soviets remained far behind the [First World](/wiki/First_World "First World") in chemistry, biology, and computer science. Under Stalin, the Soviet government persecuted [geneticists](/wiki/Geneticists "Geneticists") in favour of [Lysenkoism](/wiki/Lysenkoism "Lysenkoism"), a [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience") rejected by the scientific community in the Soviet Union and abroad but supported by Stalin's inner circles. Implemented in the USSR and China, it resulted in reduced crop yields and is widely believed to have contributed to the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine").[[356]](#cite_note-356) In the 1980s, the Soviet Union had more [scientists](/wiki/Scientists "Scientists") and [engineers](/wiki/Engineers "Engineers") relative to the world's population than any other major country, owing to strong levels of state support.[[357]](#cite_note-357) Some of its most remarkable technological achievements, such as launching the [world's first space satellite](/wiki/Sputnik_1 "Sputnik 1"), were achieved through military research.[[358]](#cite_note-Library_of_Congress_Country_Studies-358)

On housing the main problem was over-crowding rather than homelessness in the socialist planned economies. In the USSR the area of residential accommodation was 15.5 square meters per person by 1990 in urban areas but 15 percent of the population were without their own separate accommodation and had to live in communal apartments according to the 1989 census.[[359]](#cite_note-359) Housing was generally of good quality in both the CEE/CIS region and in the OECD countries: 98 and 99 percent of the population in the OECD countries had access to safe drinking water and improved sanitation respectively, compared to 93 and 85 percent in the CEE/CIS area by 1990.[[360]](#cite_note-360)

Unemployment did not exist officially in the socialist planned economies, though there were people between jobs and a fraction of unemployable people as a result of illness, disability or other problems, such as alcoholism. The proportion of people changing jobs was between 6 and 13 percent of the labour force a year according to employment data during the 1970s and 1980s in Central and Eastern Europe and the USSR. Labour exchanges were established in the USSR in 1967 to help enterprises re-allocate workers and provide information on job vacancies. Compulsory unemployment insurance schemes operated in Bulgaria, Eastern Germany and Hungary but the numbers claiming support as a result of losing their job through no fault of their own numbered a few hundred a year.[[361]](#cite_note-361)

By 1988, GDP per person, measured at purchasing power parity in US dollars, was $7,519 in Russia and $6,304 for the USSR. The highest income was to be found in Slovenia ($10,663) and Estonia ($9,078) and the lowest in Albania ($1,386) and Tajikistan ($2,730). Across the whole CEE/CIS area, GDP per person was estimated at $6,162.[[362]](#cite_note-362) This compared to the US with $20,651 and $16,006 for Germany in the same year. For the OECD area as a whole estimated GDP per person was $14,385.[[363]](#cite_note-363) Thus, on the basis of IMF estimates, national income (GDP) per person in the CEE/CIS area was 43 percent of that in the OECD area.

#### Economic problems of the Soviet planned economies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/78/Graph_of_Soviet_National_Income_Growth.png/500px-Graph_of_Soviet_National_Income_Growth.png)](/wiki/File:Graph_of_Soviet_National_Income_Growth.png)

Soviet national income 1928–1987 growth in % based on estimates of the official statistical agency of the Soviet Union, the CIA and revised estimates by [Grigorii Khanin](/wiki/Grigorii_Khanin "Grigorii Khanin")

From the 1960s onwards, CMEA countries, beginning with East Germany, attempted "intensive" growth strategies, aiming to raise the productivity of labour and capital. However, in practice this meant that investment was shifted towards new branches of industry, including the electronics, computing, automotive and nuclear power sectors, leaving the traditional heavy industries dependent upon older technologies. Despite the rhetoric about modernization, innovation remained weak as enterprise managers preferred routine production that was easier to plan and brought them predictable bonuses. Embargoes on high technology exports organized through the US-supported [CoCom](/wiki/CoCom "CoCom") arrangement hampered technology transfer. Enterprise managers also ignored inducements to introduce labour-saving measures as they wished to retain a reserve of personnel to be available to meet their production target by working at top speed when supplies were delayed.[[364]](#cite_note-364)

Under conditions of "taut planning", the economy was expected to produce a volume of output higher than the reported capacity of enterprises and there was no "slack" in the system. Enterprises faced a resource constraint and hoarded labour and other inputs and avoided sub-contracting intermediate production activities, preferring to retain the work in-house. The enterprise, according to the theory promulgated by [János Kornai](/wiki/J%C3%A1nos_Kornai "János Kornai"), was constrained by its resources not by the demand for its goods and services; nor was it constrained by its finances since the government was not likely to shut it down if it failed to meet its financial targets. Enterprises in socialist planned economies operated within a "soft" budget constraint, unlike enterprises in capitalist market economies which are demand-constrained and operate within "hard" budget constraints, as they face bankruptcy if their costs exceed their sales. As all producers were working in a resource-constrained economy they were perpetually in short supply and the shortages could never be eliminated, leading to chronic disruption of production schedules. The effect of this was to preserve a high level of employment.[[365]](#cite_note-365)

As the supply of consumer goods failed to match rising incomes (because workers still received their pay even if they were not fully productive), household savings accumulated, indicating, in the official terminology, "postponed demand". Western economists called this "[monetary overhang](/wiki/Monetary_overhang "Monetary overhang")" or "repressed inflation". Prices on the black market were several times higher than in the official price-controlled outlets, reflecting the scarcity and possible illegality of the sale of these items. Therefore, although consumer welfare was reduced by shortages, the prices households paid for their regular consumption were lower than would have been the case had prices been set at market-clearing levels.[[366]](#cite_note-366)

Over the course of the 1980s it became clear that the CMEA area was "in crisis", although it remained viable economically and was not expected to collapse.[[367]](#cite_note-367) The "extensive" growth model was retarding growth in the CMEA as a whole, with member countries dependent upon supplies of raw materials from the USSR and upon the Soviet market for sales of goods. The decline in growth rates reflected a combination of diminishing returns to capital accumulation and low innovation as well as micro-economic inefficiencies, which a high rate of saving and investment was unable to counter. The CMEA was supposed to ensure coordination of national plans but it failed even to develop a common methodology for planning which could be adopted by its member states. As each member state was reluctant to give up national self-sufficiency the CMEA's efforts to encourage specialization was thwarted. There were very few joint ventures and therefore little intra-enterprise technology transfer and trade, which in the capitalist world was often undertaken by trans-national corporations. The International Bank for Economic Cooperation had no means of converting a country's trade surplus into an option to buy goods and services from other CMEA members.[[368]](#cite_note-368)

## Public views

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Socialist_Alternative_Protest1.jpg/250px-Socialist_Alternative_Protest1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Socialist_Alternative_Protest1.jpg)

[Socialist Alternative](/wiki/Socialist_Alternative_(United_States) "Socialist Alternative (United States)") holding a mass anti-war protest, 2007

A multitude of polls have found significant levels of support for socialism among modern populations.[[369]](#cite_note-369)[[370]](#cite_note-370)

A 2018 [IPSOS](/wiki/Ipsos "Ipsos") poll found that 50% of the respondents globally strongly or somewhat agreed that present socialist values were of great value for societal progress. In China this was 84%, India 72%, Malaysia 62%, Turkey 62%, South Africa 57%, Brazil 57%, Russia 55%, Spain 54%, Argentina 52%, Mexico 51%, Saudi Arabia 51%, Sweden 49%, Canada 49%, Great Britain 49%, Australia 49%, Poland 48%, Chile 48%, South Korea 48%, Peru 48%, Italy 47%, Serbia 47%, Germany 45%, Belgium 44%, Romania 40%, United States 39%, France 31%, Hungary 28% and Japan 21%.[[371]](#cite_note-371)

A 2021 survey conducted by the [Institute of Economic Affairs](/wiki/Institute_of_Economic_Affairs "Institute of Economic Affairs") (IEA) found that 67% of young British (16–24) respondents wanted to live in a [socialist economic system](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy"), 72% supported the [re-nationalisation](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") of various industries such as [energy](/wiki/Energy_industry "Energy industry"), [water](/wiki/Water_industry "Water industry") along with [railways](/wiki/Railways "Railways") and 75% agreed with the view that [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") was a specifically [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalist "Capitalist") problem.[[372]](#cite_note-372)

A 2023 IPSOS poll found that a majority of British public favoured public ownership of [utilities](/wiki/Utilities "Utilities") including water, rail and [electricity](/wiki/Electricity "Electricity"). Specifically, 68% of respondents favoured the nationalisation of water services, 65% supporting the nationalisation of the railways, 63% supporting the public ownership of [power networks](/wiki/Electrical_grid "Electrical grid") and 39% favouring [broadband access](/wiki/Broadband_access "Broadband access") which is operated by the government. The poll also found broad levels of support among traditional Labour and Conservative voters.[[373]](#cite_note-373)

The results of a 2019 [Axios](/wiki/Axios_(website) "Axios (website)") poll found that 70% of US [millennials](/wiki/Millennials "Millennials") were willing to vote for a socialist candidate and 50% of this same demographic had a somewhat or very unfavourable view of capitalism.[[374]](#cite_note-374) Subsequently, another 2021 Axios poll found that 51% of 18–34 US adults had a positive view of socialism. Yet, 41% of Americans more generally had a positive view of socialism compared to 52% of those who viewing socialism more negatively.[[375]](#cite_note-375)

In 2023, the [Fraser Institute](/wiki/Fraser_Institute "Fraser Institute") published findings which found that 42% of Canadians viewed socialism as the ideal system compared to 43% of British respondents, 40% Australian respondents and 31% American respondents. Overall support for socialism ranged from 50% of Canadians 18–24 year olds to 28% of Canadians over 55.[[376]](#cite_note-376)

In 2025, a joint [Rasmussen](/wiki/Rasmussen_Reports "Rasmussen Reports") and Heartland Institute poll had found that 53% of young American voters (18–39 age range) supported the prospect of a socialist candidate running in the upcoming 2028 presidential election and 76% of this same demographic favoured the nationalisation of key industries such as energy, healthcare and big tech sectors.[[377]](#cite_note-377)

## Criticism

Main article: [Criticism of socialism](/wiki/Criticism_of_socialism "Criticism of socialism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Portrait_of_Milton_Friedman.jpg/120px-Portrait_of_Milton_Friedman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Milton_Friedman.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Ludwig_von_Mises.jpg/120px-Ludwig_von_Mises.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ludwig_von_Mises.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Friedrich_Hayek_portrait.jpg/120px-Friedrich_Hayek_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedrich_Hayek_portrait.jpg)

L–R: [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman"), [Ludwig von Mises](/wiki/Ludwig_von_Mises "Ludwig von Mises") and [Friedrich Hayek](/wiki/Friedrich_Hayek "Friedrich Hayek"), economists belonging to the [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_school_of_economics "Chicago school of economics") and [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_school_of_economics "Austrian school of economics") schools of economics and critics of socialism in the 20th century

According to [analytical Marxist](/wiki/Analytical_Marxist "Analytical Marxist") sociologist [Erik Olin Wright](/wiki/Erik_Olin_Wright "Erik Olin Wright"), "The Right condemned socialism as violating individual rights to private property and unleashing monstrous forms of state oppression", while "the Left saw it as opening up new vistas of social equality, genuine freedom and the development of human potentials."[[378]](#cite_note-378)

Because of socialism's many varieties, most critiques have focused on a specific approach. Proponents of one approach typically criticise others. Socialism has been criticised in terms of its [models of economic organization](/wiki/Socialist_economics "Socialist economics") as well as its political and social implications. Other critiques are directed at the [socialist movement](/wiki/Socialist_movement "Socialist movement"), [parties](/wiki/Socialist_Party "Socialist Party"), or existing [states](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state").

Some forms of criticism occupy theoretical grounds, such as in the [economic calculation problem](/wiki/Economic_calculation_problem "Economic calculation problem") presented by proponents of the [Austrian School](/wiki/Austrian_School "Austrian School") as part of the [socialist calculation debate](/wiki/Socialist_calculation_debate "Socialist calculation debate"), while others support their criticism by examining historical attempts to establish socialist societies. The economic calculation problem concerns the feasibility and methods of resource allocation for a [planned socialist system](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy").[[379]](#cite_note-379)[[380]](#cite_note-380)[[381]](#cite_note-381) Central planning is also criticized by elements of the radical left. Libertarian socialist economist [Robin Hahnel](/wiki/Robin_Hahnel "Robin Hahnel") notes that even if central planning overcame its inherent inhibitions of incentives and innovation, it would nevertheless be unable to maximize economic democracy and self-management, which he believes are concepts that are more intellectually coherent, consistent and just than mainstream notions of economic freedom.[[382]](#cite_note-Hahnel,_Robin_2002-382)

[Economic liberals](/wiki/Economic_liberals "Economic liberals") and [right-libertarians](/wiki/Right-libertarians "Right-libertarians") argue that [private ownership](/wiki/Private_enterprise "Private enterprise") of the [means of production](/wiki/Means_of_production "Means of production") and market exchange are natural entities or moral rights which are central to freedom and liberty and argue that the economic dynamics of [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") are immutable and absolute. As such, they also argue that [public ownership](/wiki/Public_ownership "Public ownership") of the means of production and [economic planning](/wiki/Economic_planning "Economic planning") are infringements upon liberty.[[383]](#cite_note-milton2-383)[[384]](#cite_note-Bellamy,_Richard_2003_60-384)

Critics of socialism have argued that in any society where everyone holds equal wealth, there can be no material incentive to work because one does not receive rewards for a work well done. They further argue that incentives increase productivity for all people and that the loss of those effects would lead to stagnation. Some critics of socialism argue that income sharing reduces individual incentives to work and therefore incomes should be individualized as much as possible.[[385]](#cite_note-385)

[Peter Self](/wiki/Peter_Self "Peter Self") criticized the aims of socialism, arguing that equality erodes away at individual diversities and that the establishment of an equal society would have to entail strong coercion.[[386]](#cite_note-Self-386)

[Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman") argued that the absence of private economic activity would enable political leaders to grant themselves coercive powers, powers that, under a capitalist system, would instead be granted by a capitalist class, which Friedman found preferable.[[387]](#cite_note-Bellamy,_Richard_2003_602-387)

Many commentators on the political right point to the [mass killings under communist regimes](/wiki/Mass_killings_under_communist_regimes "Mass killings under communist regimes"), claiming them as an indictment of socialism.[[388]](#cite_note-388)[[389]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEngel-DiMauro20211–17-389)[[390]](#cite_note-390) Opponents of this view, including supporters of socialism, state that these killings were aberrations caused by specific authoritarian regimes rather than socialism itself. They draw comparisons to killings, famines and excess deaths under capitalism, [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") and [anti-communist authoritarian governments](/wiki/Anti-communist_mass_killings "Anti-communist mass killings").[[391]](#cite_note-391)
